About the Salary Data in This Guide
Every career profile in this guide includes a Nepal salary breakdown (entry / mid / senior level) and an
international comparison across three major foreign employment regions. These figures are benchmarked
ranges built from real, publicly published 2025-2026 salary research rather than invented estimates.
Sources: Nepal figures compiled from SalaryNepal.com, Kumarijob, Merokalam and CloudColleague 2026
salary guides plus PayScale/Glassdoor Nepal listings; Gulf figures from Expatica, Huduri, and Tuscan Consulting
GCC Compensation & Benefits Survey 2026; Western figures from the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics
Occupational Employment & Wage Statistics (May 2025 release); Australia figures from the Australian Bureau
of Statistics Average Weekly Earnings (Nov 2025) and FairWorkMate/CloudColleague 2026 industry
breakdowns. Figures are benchmarked ranges, not individual job-specific quotes, and actual pay will vary by
employer, city and negotiation.
A note on interpretation: salary ranges represent typical market benchmarks for each broad career category,
adjusted per role. Individual pay will always vary based on the specific employer, city, negotiation,
qualifications, and market conditions at the time of hiring. These figures should be used as a directional guide
for career planning, not as a guaranteed offer.

001
Software Engineer
OVERVIEW
A Software Engineer is a professional working within the Technology & IT sector, and in simple terms, this
role means the following: Designs, builds, and maintains software applications and systems that power
everyday digital life. While the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific
combination of daily tasks, technical or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students
can plan around from an early stage. This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are
drawn to technology & it because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the
right foundational skills are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Software Engineer typically spends their time
applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to technology & it. Early in a career, most
of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in this role
usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
One of the most in-demand careers worldwide, with strong remote-work opportunities. Beyond that
immediate appeal, this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense
of clear purpose and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities,
companies, or the country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth
noting that a career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many
professionals in this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually
through coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Nepal's tech ecosystem is expanding quickly, with Kathmandu-based
startups, outsourcing companies, and remote-first international employers all competing for skilled talent.
The rise of AI tools has only accelerated hiring, and many companies abroad now hire Nepali developers,
designers, and analysts to work fully remotely without ever relocating. Taken together, these trends
suggest that a career as a Software Engineer is likely to remain relevant and employable for the
foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's
skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Software Engineer in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 28,000 - 42,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 60,000 - 96,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
TECHNOLOGY & IT
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 5 of 1372

have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
— earnings can rise to NPR 179,500 - 305,500 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Software Engineer-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia): approximately
USD 2,200 - 11,700 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 5,000 - 19,450 / month;
Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 3,800 - 9,750 / month. As these figures show, Gulf countries
generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to zero income
tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and Western Europe)
tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall in between,
combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth remembering that
higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa and relocation costs,
and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Software Engineer, the core skills generally required
include Programming, Problem Solving, and DSA. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills, most
professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost every role
— even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the
public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep
learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced professionals as being just as
important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree certificate.
Programming
Problem Solving
DSA
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in technology & it, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations or fully
independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Software Engineer offers a genuinely solid
combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to seek
out an internship, a small real-world project, or direct conversations with working professionals in this
field to get a clearer, first-hand sense of whether this path truly fits your interests and strengths.
ENTRY
NPR 28,000 - 42,000 / month
MID
NPR 60,000 - 96,000 / month
SENIOR
NPR 179,500 - 305,500 / month
Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia)
USD 2,200 - 11,700 / month
USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe
USD 5,000 - 19,450 / month
Australia & New Zealand
USD 3,800 - 9,750 / month

002
Full Stack Web Developer
OVERVIEW
A Full Stack Web Developer is a professional working within the Technology & IT sector, and in simple
terms, this role means the following: Builds both the frontend and backend of websites and applications
from start to finish. While the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific
combination of daily tasks, technical or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students
can plan around from an early stage. This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are
drawn to technology & it because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the

freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.

The single most requested skill among startups and freelance clients today. Beyond that immediate

003
Mobile App Developer
OVERVIEW
A Mobile App Developer is a professional working within the Technology & IT sector, and in simple terms,
this role means the following: Designs and develops mobile applications for Android and iOS platforms.
While the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks,
technical or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an
early stage. This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to technology & it
because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills

Launching your own app on the Play Store can even become a source of passive income. Beyond

004
Data Scientist
OVERVIEW
A Data Scientist is a professional working within the Technology & IT sector, and in simple terms, this role
means the following: Extracts patterns and insights from large datasets to guide business decisions. While
the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical
or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage.
This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to technology & it because it
offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built
during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Data Scientist typically spends their time

sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
One of the fastest-growing and highest-paid fields in the current AI-driven economy. Beyond that
immediate appeal, this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense
of clear purpose and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities,
companies, or the country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth
noting that a career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many
professionals in this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually
through coursework, internships, and early job experience.

005
AI/ML Engineer

noting that a career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many
professionals in this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually
through coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Nepal's tech ecosystem is expanding quickly, with Kathmandu-based
startups, outsourcing companies, and remote-first international employers all competing for skilled talent.
The rise of AI tools has only accelerated hiring, and many companies abroad now hire Nepali developers,
designers, and analysts to work fully remotely without ever relocating. Taken together, these trends
suggest that a career as a AI/ML Engineer is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable
future, though as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather
than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a AI/ML Engineer in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 29,500 - 44,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 63,000 - 101,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
TECHNOLOGY & IT
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 15 of 1372

have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
— earnings can rise to NPR 189,500 - 322,500 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a AI/ML
Engineer-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia): approximately USD
2,300 - 12,350 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 5,250 - 20,550 / month;
Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 4,000 - 10,250 / month. As these figures show, Gulf countries
generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to zero income
tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and Western Europe)
tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall in between,
combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth remembering that
higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa and relocation costs,
and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a AI/ML Engineer, the core skills generally required include
Python, ML Frameworks, and Math. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills, most professionals who
do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost every role — even highly
technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the public. Time
management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep learning
throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced professionals as being just as
important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree certificate.
Python
ML Frameworks
Math
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in technology & it, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations or fully
independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a AI/ML Engineer offers a genuinely solid
combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to seek
out an internship, a small real-world project, or direct conversations with working professionals in this
field to get a clearer, first-hand sense of whether this path truly fits your interests and strengths.

006
Cybersecurity Analyst
OVERVIEW
A Cybersecurity Analyst is a professional working within the Technology & IT sector, and in simple terms,
this role means the following: Protects companies and systems from hacking, data breaches, and cyber
threats. While the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily
tasks, technical or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around
from an early stage. This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to
technology & it because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right
foundational skills are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Cybersecurity Analyst typically spends their time
applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to technology & it. Early in a career, most
of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in this role
usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
As Nepal goes digital, the shortage of skilled cybersecurity experts creates huge opportunity. Beyond
that immediate appeal, this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a
sense of clear purpose and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities,
companies, or the country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth
noting that a career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many
professionals in this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually
through coursework, internships, and early job experience.

designers, and analysts to work fully remotely without ever relocating. Taken together, these trends

On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Game Developer typically spends their time
applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to technology & it. Early in a career, most
of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in this role
usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
The global gaming industry keeps growing, and Nepali developers can work with studios worldwide.
Beyond that immediate appeal, this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely
value: a sense of clear purpose and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients,
communities, companies, or the country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different
options, it is worth noting that a career like this does not require having everything figured out
immediately — many professionals in this field started with only a general interest and built specific
expertise gradually through coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Nepal's tech ecosystem is expanding quickly, with Kathmandu-based
startups, outsourcing companies, and remote-first international employers all competing for skilled talent.
The rise of AI tools has only accelerated hiring, and many companies abroad now hire Nepali developers,
designers, and analysts to work fully remotely without ever relocating. Taken together, these trends
suggest that a career as a Game Developer is likely to remain relevant and employable for the
foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's
skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Game Developer in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 32,000 - 48,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 68,500 - 109,500 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
TECHNOLOGY & IT
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 26 of 1372

have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
— earnings can rise to NPR 205,000 - 348,500 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Game Developer-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia): approximately
USD 2,500 - 13,350 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 5,700 - 22,200 / month;
Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 4,350 - 11,100 / month. As these figures show, Gulf countries
generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to zero income
tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and Western Europe)
tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall in between,
combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth remembering that
higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa and relocation costs,
and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Game Developer, the core skills generally required include
Unity/Unreal, C#, and Game Design. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills, most professionals who
do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost every role — even highly
technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the public. Time
management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep learning
throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced professionals as being just as
important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree certificate.
Unity/Unreal
C#
Game Design
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in technology & it, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations or fully
independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Game Developer offers a genuinely solid
combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to seek
out an internship, a small real-world project, or direct conversations with working professionals in this
field to get a clearer, first-hand sense of whether this path truly fits your interests and strengths.

015
IT Support Specialist
OVERVIEW
A IT Support Specialist is a professional working within the Technology & IT sector, and in simple terms, this
role means the following: Resolves computer and software issues for organizations and their employees.
While the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks,
technical or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an
early stage. This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to technology & it
because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills
are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a IT Support Specialist typically spends their time
applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to technology & it. Early in a career, most
of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in this role
usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
One of the easiest and fastest ways to start a career in IT. Beyond that immediate appeal, this career
also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose and visible
impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the country as a
whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a career like this
does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in this field
started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through coursework,
internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Nepal's tech ecosystem is expanding quickly, with Kathmandu-based
startups, outsourcing companies, and remote-first international employers all competing for skilled talent.
The rise of AI tools has only accelerated hiring, and many companies abroad now hire Nepali developers,
designers, and analysts to work fully remotely without ever relocating. Taken together, these trends
suggest that a career as a IT Support Specialist is likely to remain relevant and employable for the
foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's
skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a IT Support Specialist in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 29,500 - 44,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 63,500 - 101,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
TECHNOLOGY & IT
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 40 of 1372

have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
— earnings can rise to NPR 190,000 - 322,500 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a IT
Support Specialist-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia):
approximately USD 2,300 - 12,350 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 5,250 -
20,550 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 4,000 - 10,300 / month. As these figures
show, Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad
thanks to zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK,
and Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically
fall in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a IT Support Specialist, the core skills generally required
include Troubleshooting, Communication, and Hardware Basics. Beyond these technical or role-specific
skills, most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost
every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues,
clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine
willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.
Troubleshooting
Communication
Hardware Basics
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in technology & it, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations or fully
independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a IT Support Specialist offers a genuinely
solid combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to
ENTRY

MID

Civil Engineer
OVERVIEW
A Civil Engineer is a professional working within the Engineering sector, and in simple terms, this role
means the following: Designs and oversees the construction of buildings, roads, and bridges. While the
title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or
people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage.
This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to engineering because it offers a
fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built during
college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Civil Engineer typically spends their time applying
specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with colleagues, clients,
supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying current with the latest
tools, standards, or best practices relevant to engineering. Early in a career, most of the work tends to be
more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in this role usually take on
responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and sometimes managing
entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending on whether someone
works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/freelance professional,
but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.

Nepal's ongoing infrastructure boom means steady, long-term demand for civil engineers. Beyond

sense of clear purpose and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities,
companies, or the country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth
noting that a career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many
professionals in this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually
through coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: With continuous investment in roads, hydropower, urban housing, and
public infrastructure, Nepal's engineering sector remains one of the more dependable fields for steady,
long-term employment. Government contracts, private construction firms, and international development
projects all regularly hire engineers across every specialization. Taken together, these trends suggest that
a career as a Civil Engineer is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though
as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying
solely on a single degree or qualification.

tend to earn closer to NPR 43,500 - 69,500 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who

common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in engineering, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations or fully
independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Computer/Electronics Engineer offers a
genuinely solid combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is

ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 53 of 1372

020
Architect
OVERVIEW
A Architect is a professional working within the Engineering sector, and in simple terms, this role means
the following: Designs buildings and spaces that blend creativity with structural engineering. While the title
itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or
people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage.
This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to engineering because it offers a
fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built during
college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Architect typically spends their time applying
specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with colleagues, clients,
supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying current with the latest
tools, standards, or best practices relevant to engineering. Early in a career, most of the work tends to be
more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in this role usually take on
responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and sometimes managing
entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending on whether someone
works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/freelance professional,
but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A dream career for students who love combining art with technical precision. Beyond that
immediate appeal, this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense
of clear purpose and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities,
companies, or the country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth
noting that a career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many
professionals in this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually
through coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: With continuous investment in roads, hydropower, urban housing, and
public infrastructure, Nepal's engineering sector remains one of the more dependable fields for steady,
long-term employment. Government contracts, private construction firms, and international development
projects all regularly hire engineers across every specialization. Taken together, these trends suggest that
a career as a Architect is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though as
with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely
on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Architect in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable progression
tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry level, fresh
graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around NPR 27,500
- 41,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role tend to
earn closer to NPR 50,500 - 81,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who have
ENGINEERING
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 54 of 1372

built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice —
earnings can rise to NPR 126,500 - 215,500 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Architect-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia): approximately USD
1,900 - 9,600 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 4,750 - 13,050 / month;
Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 3,800 - 9,900 / month. As these figures show, Gulf countries
generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to zero income
tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and Western Europe)
tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall in between,
combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth remembering that
higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa and relocation costs,
and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Architect, the core skills generally required include Design
Software, Creativity, and Structural Basics. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills, most
professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost every role
— even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the
public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep
learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced professionals as being just as
important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree certificate.
Design Software
Creativity
Structural Basics
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in engineering, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations or fully
independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Architect offers a genuinely solid
combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to seek
out an internship, a small real-world project, or direct conversations with working professionals in this
field to get a clearer, first-hand sense of whether this path truly fits your interests and strengths.

colleagues, clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a
genuine willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.

common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in engineering, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations or fully
independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Environmental Engineer offers a genuinely
solid combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to

025
Hydropower Engineer
OVERVIEW
A Hydropower Engineer is a professional working within the Engineering sector, and in simple terms, this
role means the following: Designs systems that convert river water into electricity. While the title itself may
sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or people-facing
skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage. This career path
is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to engineering because it offers a fairly clear route
from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built during college years and
early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Hydropower Engineer typically spends their time
applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to engineering. Early in a career, most of
the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in this role
usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A core career for Nepal, harnessing its greatest natural resource — water. Beyond that immediate
appeal, this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear
purpose and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or
the country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: With continuous investment in roads, hydropower, urban housing, and
public infrastructure, Nepal's engineering sector remains one of the more dependable fields for steady,
long-term employment. Government contracts, private construction firms, and international development
projects all regularly hire engineers across every specialization. Taken together, these trends suggest that
a career as a Hydropower Engineer is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future,
though as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than
relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Hydropower Engineer in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 25,500 - 38,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 47,500 - 76,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
ENGINEERING
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 67 of 1372

have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
— earnings can rise to NPR 118,500 - 201,500 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Hydropower Engineer-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia):
approximately USD 1,800 - 9,000 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 4,450 -
12,200 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 3,550 - 9,250 / month. As these figures show,
Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to
zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and
Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall
in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Hydropower Engineer, the core skills generally required
include Fluid Mechanics, Power Systems, and Site Assessment. Beyond these technical or role-specific
skills, most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost
every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues,
clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine
willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.
Fluid Mechanics
Power Systems
Site Assessment

Industrial Engineer
OVERVIEW
A Industrial Engineer is a professional working within the Engineering sector, and in simple terms, this role
means the following: Improves factory processes and systems to make production more efficient. While
the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical
or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage.
This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to engineering because it offers a

title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or
people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage.

zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and
Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall
in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Telecommunications Engineer, the core skills generally
required include Networking, Signal Processing, and RF Systems. Beyond these technical or role-specific
skills, most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost
every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues,
clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine
willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.
Networking
Signal Processing
RF Systems
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in engineering, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations or fully
independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Telecommunications Engineer offers a
genuinely solid combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is

030
Mining Engineer
OVERVIEW
A Mining Engineer is a professional working within the Engineering sector, and in simple terms, this role
means the following: Designs the process of extracting valuable resources from mines. While the title itself
may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or people-
facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage. This
career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to engineering because it offers a
fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built during
college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Mining Engineer typically spends their time
applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to engineering. Early in a career, most of
the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in this role
usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
An underrated field for exploring Nepal's untapped mineral resources. Beyond that immediate
appeal, this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear
purpose and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or
the country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: With continuous investment in roads, hydropower, urban housing, and
public infrastructure, Nepal's engineering sector remains one of the more dependable fields for steady,
long-term employment. Government contracts, private construction firms, and international development
projects all regularly hire engineers across every specialization. Taken together, these trends suggest that
a career as a Mining Engineer is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future,
though as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than
relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Mining Engineer in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 24,000 - 36,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 44,500 - 71,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
ENGINEERING
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 82 of 1372

have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
— earnings can rise to NPR 111,000 - 189,000 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Mining Engineer-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia): approximately
USD 1,650 - 8,450 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 4,150 - 11,450 / month;
Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 3,350 - 8,650 / month. As these figures show, Gulf countries
generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to zero income
tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and Western Europe)
tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall in between,
combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth remembering that
higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa and relocation costs,
and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Mining Engineer, the core skills generally required include
Geology, Safety Systems, and Extraction Methods. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills, most
professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost every role
— even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the
public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep
learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced professionals as being just as
important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree certificate.
Geology
Safety Systems
Extraction Methods
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in engineering, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations or fully
independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Mining Engineer offers a genuinely solid
combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to seek
out an internship, a small real-world project, or direct conversations with working professionals in this
field to get a clearer, first-hand sense of whether this path truly fits your interests and strengths.

Medical Doctor (MBBS)
OVERVIEW
A Medical Doctor (MBBS) is a professional working within the Medical & Healthcare sector, and in simple
terms, this role means the following: Examines patients, diagnoses illness, and provides medical
treatment. While the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of
daily tasks, technical or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan
around from an early stage. This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to
medical & healthcare because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the
right foundational skills are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Medical Doctor (MBBS) typically spends their time
applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to medical & healthcare. Early in a
career, most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior
professionals in this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring
junior staff, and sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary
significantly depending on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO,
or as an independent/freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely
consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
One of Nepal's most respected careers, directly helping and healing society. Beyond that immediate
appeal, this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear
purpose and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or
the country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Nepal's healthcare sector spans government hospitals, a fast-growing
private hospital and clinic industry, and an increasing number of international recruitment agencies
placing Nepali healthcare workers abroad. Public health awareness and medical tourism are also
contributing to sector growth. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a Medical Doctor
(MBBS) is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though as with any field,
staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a single
degree or qualification.

NPR 40,000 - 60,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role

SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Nurse-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia): approximately USD 2,500 -
13,250 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 5,650 - 29,400 / month; Australia &
New Zealand: approximately USD 4,050 - 20,300 / month. As these figures show, Gulf countries generally
offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to zero income tax and
employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and Western Europe) tend to
offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall in between, combining
strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth remembering that higher foreign
salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa and relocation costs, and time
away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Nurse, the core skills generally required include Patient
Care, Medical Procedures, and Compassion. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills, most
professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost every role
— even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the
public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep
learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced professionals as being just as
important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree certificate.
Patient Care
Medical Procedures
Compassion
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in medical & healthcare, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations
or fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Nurse offers a genuinely solid combination
of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to seek out an
internship, a small real-world project, or direct conversations with working professionals in this field to
get a clearer, first-hand sense of whether this path truly fits your interests and strengths.
ENTRY

MID
NPR 73,500 - 117,500 / month
SENIOR
NPR 249,000 - 423,000 / month
Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia)

035
Physiotherapist
OVERVIEW
A Physiotherapist is a professional working within the Medical & Healthcare sector, and in simple terms,
this role means the following: Uses exercise-based therapy to help patients recover from injury or
disability. While the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily
tasks, technical or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around
from an early stage. This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to medical &
healthcare because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right
foundational skills are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Physiotherapist typically spends their time
applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to medical & healthcare. Early in a
career, most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior
professionals in this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring
junior staff, and sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary
significantly depending on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO,
or as an independent/freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely
consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A great career combining sports, rehabilitation centers, and hands-on patient care. Beyond that
immediate appeal, this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense
of clear purpose and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities,
companies, or the country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth
noting that a career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many
professionals in this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually
through coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Nepal's healthcare sector spans government hospitals, a fast-growing
private hospital and clinic industry, and an increasing number of international recruitment agencies
placing Nepali healthcare workers abroad. Public health awareness and medical tourism are also
contributing to sector growth. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a Physiotherapist is
likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying
competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or
qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Physiotherapist in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 33,000 - 50,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
MEDICAL & HEALTHCARE
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 93 of 1372

tend to earn closer to NPR 62,000 - 99,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
— earnings can rise to NPR 209,000 - 355,500 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.

Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand

Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 3,400 - 17,050 / month. As these figures show, Gulf countries

every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues,
clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine

contributing to sector growth. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a Nutritionist/Dietitian
is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying
competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or
qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Nutritionist/Dietitian in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 39,500 - 59,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
MEDICAL & HEALTHCARE
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 105 of 1372

tend to earn closer to NPR 73,500 - 117,500 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
— earnings can rise to NPR 249,000 - 423,000 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Nutritionist/Dietitian-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia):
approximately USD 2,500 - 13,250 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 5,650 -
29,400 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 4,050 - 20,300 / month. As these figures
show, Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad
thanks to zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK,
and Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically
fall in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Nutritionist/Dietitian, the core skills generally required
include Nutrition Science, Communication, and Meal Planning. Beyond these technical or role-specific
skills, most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost
every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues,
clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine
willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.
Nutrition Science
Communication

CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in medical & healthcare, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations
or fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.

040
Psychologist/Counselor
OVERVIEW
A Psychologist/Counselor is a professional working within the Medical & Healthcare sector, and in simple
terms, this role means the following: Provides counseling and therapy to support mental health and well-
being. While the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily
tasks, technical or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around
from an early stage. This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to medical &
healthcare because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right
foundational skills are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Psychologist/Counselor typically spends their
time applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to medical & healthcare. Early in a
career, most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior
professionals in this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring
junior staff, and sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary
significantly depending on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO,
or as an independent/freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely
consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
As mental health awareness grows in Nepal, so does the need for this career. Beyond that immediate
appeal, this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear
purpose and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or
the country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Nepal's healthcare sector spans government hospitals, a fast-growing
private hospital and clinic industry, and an increasing number of international recruitment agencies
placing Nepali healthcare workers abroad. Public health awareness and medical tourism are also
contributing to sector growth. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a Psychologist/
Counselor is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though as with any field,
staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a single
degree or qualification.

Chartered Accountant (CA)
OVERVIEW
A Chartered Accountant (CA) is a professional working within the Business & Finance sector, and in simple
terms, this role means the following: Manages company accounts, taxes, and financial audits. While the
title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or
people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage.
This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to business & finance because it
offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built
during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Chartered Accountant (CA) typically spends their
time applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying

companies and startups create consistent demand for finance and business professionals. The rise of
fintech, digital payments, and a maturing stock market (NEPSE) are opening entirely new sub-fields within
this space. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a Chartered Accountant (CA) is likely to
remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive
will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
