applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to business & finance. Early in a career,
most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in
this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
Banks, investment firms, and corporations all have high demand for this skill. Beyond that immediate
appeal, this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear
purpose and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or
the country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Nepal's banking sector, insurance industry, and growing base of private
companies and startups create consistent demand for finance and business professionals. The rise of
fintech, digital payments, and a maturing stock market (NEPSE) are opening entirely new sub-fields within
this space. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a Financial Analyst is likely to remain
relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive will
require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Financial Analyst in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 26,000 - 39,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 51,500 - 82,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
BUSINESS & FINANCE
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 119 of 1372

— earnings can rise to NPR 149,000 - 253,500 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Financial Analyst-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia): approximately
USD 2,050 - 10,300 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 4,450 - 15,150 / month;
Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 3,350 - 10,050 / month. As these figures show, Gulf countries
generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to zero income
tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and Western Europe)
tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall in between,
combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth remembering that
higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa and relocation costs,
and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Financial Analyst, the core skills generally required include
Excel, Financial Modeling, and Analysis. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills, most professionals
who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost every role — even highly
technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the public. Time
management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep learning
throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced professionals as being just as
important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree certificate.
Excel
Financial Modeling
Analysis
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in business & finance, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations or
fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Financial Analyst offers a genuinely solid
combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to seek
out an internship, a small real-world project, or direct conversations with working professionals in this
field to get a clearer, first-hand sense of whether this path truly fits your interests and strengths.

045
Investment Banker
OVERVIEW
A Investment Banker is a professional working within the Business & Finance sector, and in simple terms,
this role means the following: Helps companies raise capital and manage mergers and acquisitions.
While the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks,
technical or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an
early stage. This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to business & finance
because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills
are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Investment Banker typically spends their time
applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to business & finance. Early in a career,
most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in
this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A fast-paced, high-paying career offering global exposure. Beyond that immediate appeal, this
career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose and
visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the country
as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a career like
this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in this field
started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through coursework,
internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Nepal's banking sector, insurance industry, and growing base of private
companies and startups create consistent demand for finance and business professionals. The rise of
fintech, digital payments, and a maturing stock market (NEPSE) are opening entirely new sub-fields within
this space. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a Investment Banker is likely to remain
relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive will
require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.

Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Investment Banker in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry

NPR 31,000 - 46,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 61,000 - 98,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
BUSINESS & FINANCE

— earnings can rise to NPR 178,000 - 302,500 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,

important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree certificate.

OVERVIEW
A Human Resources Manager is a professional working within the Business & Finance sector, and in simple
terms, this role means the following: Manages hiring, employee relations, and workplace culture for
organizations. While the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of
daily tasks, technical or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan
around from an early stage. This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to
business & finance because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right
foundational skills are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Human Resources Manager typically spends their
time applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to business & finance. Early in a career,
most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in
this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
Every organization needs this role, making it a stable long-term career. Beyond that immediate
appeal, this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear
purpose and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or
the country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Nepal's banking sector, insurance industry, and growing base of private
companies and startups create consistent demand for finance and business professionals. The rise of
fintech, digital payments, and a maturing stock market (NEPSE) are opening entirely new sub-fields within
this space. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a Human Resources Manager is likely to
remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive
will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Human Resources Manager in Nepal generally follows a fairly
predictable progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector.
At the entry level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to
earn around NPR 32,000 - 48,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level
professionals in this role tend to earn closer to NPR 63,000 - 100,500 / month. At the senior or specialist
level — including those who have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their
BUSINESS & FINANCE
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 130 of 1372

own independent practice — earnings can rise to NPR 183,000 - 311,000 / month, though reaching this level
naturally takes time, consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Human Resources Manager-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia):
approximately USD 2,500 - 12,600 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 5,500 -
18,550 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 4,100 - 12,350 / month. As these figures show,
Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to
zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and
Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall
in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Human Resources Manager, the core skills generally
required include Communication, Recruitment, and Conflict Resolution. Beyond these technical or role-
specific skills, most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills,
since almost every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to
colleagues, clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a
genuine willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.
Communication
Recruitment
Conflict Resolution
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in business & finance, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations or
fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Human Resources Manager offers a
genuinely solid combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is
usually to seek out an internship, a small real-world project, or direct conversations with working

054
E-commerce Manager
OVERVIEW
A E-commerce Manager is a professional working within the Business & Finance sector, and in simple
terms, this role means the following: Manages the operations and sales of an online store. While the title
itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or
people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage.
This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to business & finance because it
offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built
during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a E-commerce Manager typically spends their time
applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to business & finance. Early in a career,
most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in
this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
Rising online shopping in Nepal is creating fresh opportunities in this space. Beyond that immediate
appeal, this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear
purpose and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or
the country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Nepal's banking sector, insurance industry, and growing base of private
companies and startups create consistent demand for finance and business professionals. The rise of
fintech, digital payments, and a maturing stock market (NEPSE) are opening entirely new sub-fields within
this space. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a E-commerce Manager is likely to
remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive
will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a E-commerce Manager in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 29,000 - 43,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 56,500 - 90,500 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
BUSINESS & FINANCE
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 142 of 1372

— earnings can rise to NPR 165,000 - 280,000 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a E-
commerce Manager-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia):
approximately USD 2,250 - 11,400 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 4,950 -
16,750 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 3,700 - 11,100 / month. As these figures show,
Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to
zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and
Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall
in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a E-commerce Manager, the core skills generally required
include E-commerce Platforms, Marketing, and Logistics. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills,
most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost
every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues,
clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine
willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.
E-commerce Platforms
Marketing
Logistics
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in business & finance, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations or
fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a E-commerce Manager offers a genuinely
solid combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to

055
Civil Service Officer (Nijamati)
OVERVIEW
A Civil Service Officer (Nijamati) is a professional working within the Government & Civil Service sector,
and in simple terms, this role means the following: Develops and implements government policy at the
administrative level. While the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific
combination of daily tasks, technical or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students
can plan around from an early stage. This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are
drawn to government & civil service because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment,
provided the right foundational skills are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Civil Service Officer (Nijamati) typically spends
their time applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to government & civil service. Early in a
career, most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior
professionals in this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring
junior staff, and sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary
significantly depending on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO,
or as an independent/freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely
consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
One of Nepal's most secure and respected government career paths. Beyond that immediate
appeal, this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear
purpose and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or
the country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Entry into government service in Nepal happens primarily through
competitive examinations conducted by the Public Service Commission (Lok Sewa Aayog), and while
competition is intense, successful candidates gain access to some of the most secure, respected, and
benefit-rich careers in the country. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a Civil Service
Officer (Nijamati) is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though as with
any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a
single degree or qualification.

Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Civil Service Officer (Nijamati) in Nepal generally follows a fairly
predictable progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector.
At the entry level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to
earn around NPR 33,500 - 50,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level
GOVERNMENT & CIVIL SERVICE

Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Army Officer, the core skills generally required include
Leadership, Physical Fitness, and Strategy. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills, most
professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost every role
— even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the
public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep
learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced professionals as being just as
important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree certificate.

common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in government & civil service, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger

term option.

059
Judge/Magistrate
OVERVIEW
A Judge/Magistrate is a professional working within the Government & Civil Service sector, and in simple
terms, this role means the following: Delivers justice by interpreting and applying the law in court. While
the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical
or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage.
This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to government & civil service
because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills
are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Judge/Magistrate typically spends their time
applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to government & civil service. Early in a
career, most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior
professionals in this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring
junior staff, and sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary
significantly depending on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO,
or as an independent/freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely
consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
One of the greatest achievements for any law student to reach. Beyond that immediate appeal, this
career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose and
visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the country
as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a career like
this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in this field
started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through coursework,
internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Entry into government service in Nepal happens primarily through
competitive examinations conducted by the Public Service Commission (Lok Sewa Aayog), and while
competition is intense, successful candidates gain access to some of the most secure, respected, and
benefit-rich careers in the country. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a Judge/
Magistrate is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though as with any field,
staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a single
degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Judge/Magistrate in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 36,500 - 54,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
GOVERNMENT & CIVIL SERVICE
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 157 of 1372

tend to earn closer to NPR 62,500 - 100,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
— earnings can rise to NPR 147,500 - 250,500 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Judge/Magistrate-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia): approximately
USD 1,700 - 8,850 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 3,950 - 11,050 / month;
Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 3,750 - 9,750 / month. As these figures show, Gulf countries
generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to zero income
tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and Western Europe)
tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall in between,
combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth remembering that
higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa and relocation costs,
and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Judge/Magistrate, the core skills generally required
include Law, Analytical Thinking, and Integrity. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills, most
professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost every role
— even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the
public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep
learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced professionals as being just as
important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree certificate.

Analytical Thinking
Integrity

Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Education Officer-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia): approximately
USD 1,650 - 8,600 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 3,850 - 10,750 / month;
Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 3,650 - 9,500 / month. As these figures show, Gulf countries
generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to zero income
tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and Western Europe)
tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall in between,
combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth remembering that
higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa and relocation costs,
and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Education Officer, the core skills generally required include
Education Policy, Management, and Communication. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills, most
professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost every role
— even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the
public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep
learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced professionals as being just as
important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree certificate.
Education Policy
Management
Communication
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in government & civil service, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger
organizations or fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-
term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Education Officer offers a genuinely solid
combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to seek

064
Election Commission Officer
OVERVIEW
A Election Commission Officer is a professional working within the Government & Civil Service sector, and
in simple terms, this role means the following: Manages the processes and logistics of national elections.
While the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks,
technical or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an
early stage. This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to government & civil
service because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right
foundational skills are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Election Commission Officer typically spends their
time applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to government & civil service. Early in a
career, most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior
professionals in this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring
junior staff, and sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary
significantly depending on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO,
or as an independent/freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely
consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A meaningful public-service role supporting Nepal's democratic system. Beyond that immediate
appeal, this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear
purpose and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or
the country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Entry into government service in Nepal happens primarily through
competitive examinations conducted by the Public Service Commission (Lok Sewa Aayog), and while
competition is intense, successful candidates gain access to some of the most secure, respected, and
benefit-rich careers in the country. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a Election
Commission Officer is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though as with
any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a
single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Election Commission Officer in Nepal generally follows a fairly
predictable progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector.
At the entry level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to
earn around NPR 34,500 - 52,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level
GOVERNMENT & CIVIL SERVICE

level — including those who have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their

level naturally takes time, consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.

School Teacher
OVERVIEW
A School Teacher is a professional working within the Education sector, and in simple terms, this role
means the following: Teaches subjects and guides students through their education. While the title itself
may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or people-
facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage. This
career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to education because it offers a fairly
clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built during college
years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a School Teacher typically spends their time
applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to education. Early in a career, most of
the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in this role
usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
One of the most impactful careers, directly shaping the next generation. Beyond that immediate
appeal, this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear
purpose and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or
the country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Nepal's education sector is being reshaped by private schools,
international curriculum providers, and a fast-growing EdTech industry, of which platforms like ScorpStudy
are a part. Traditional teaching remains vital, but new digital-first education careers are opening up
rapidly alongside it. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a School Teacher is likely to
remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive
will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.

Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a School Teacher in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry

NPR 16,500 - 25,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 38,500 - 61,500 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice

qualification.

Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Special Education Teacher in Nepal generally follows a fairly
predictable progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector.
At the entry level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to
earn around NPR 18,500 - 28,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level
professionals in this role tend to earn closer to NPR 43,500 - 69,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level
EDUCATION
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 183 of 1372

— including those who have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own
independent practice — earnings can rise to NPR 92,500 - 157,500 / month, though reaching this level
naturally takes time, consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Special Education Teacher-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia):
approximately USD 1,450 - 7,350 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 3,200 -
10,050 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 3,400 - 9,600 / month. As these figures show,
Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to
zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and
Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall
in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Special Education Teacher, the core skills generally
required include Patience, Special Techniques, and Communication. Beyond these technical or role-
specific skills, most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills,
since almost every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to
colleagues, clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a
genuine willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.
Patience
Special Techniques
Communication
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in education, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations or fully
independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Special Education Teacher offers a
genuinely solid combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is

069
Curriculum Developer
OVERVIEW
A Curriculum Developer is a professional working within the Education sector, and in simple terms, this role
means the following: Designs the school or college syllabus and course structure. While the title itself may
sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or people-facing
skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage. This career path
is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to education because it offers a fairly clear route
from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built during college years and
early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Curriculum Developer typically spends their time
applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to education. Early in a career, most of
the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in this role
usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A policy-level role with the power to improve entire education systems. Beyond that immediate
appeal, this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear
purpose and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or
the country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Nepal's education sector is being reshaped by private schools,
international curriculum providers, and a fast-growing EdTech industry, of which platforms like ScorpStudy
are a part. Traditional teaching remains vital, but new digital-first education careers are opening up
rapidly alongside it. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a Curriculum Developer is likely
to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying
competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or
qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Curriculum Developer in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 20,000 - 30,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 47,000 - 75,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
EDUCATION
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 186 of 1372

have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
— earnings can rise to NPR 100,500 - 170,500 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.

Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a

10,850 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 3,700 - 10,350 / month. As these figures show,

SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Curriculum Developer, the core skills generally required

skills, most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost
every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues,
clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine
willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced

as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a career like
this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in this field
started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through coursework,
internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: As Nepal continues to develop its corporate regulatory framework,
intellectual property law, and human rights institutions, the legal profession is evolving well beyond
traditional courtroom litigation into corporate advisory, compliance, and policy work. Taken together,
these trends suggest that a career as a Advocate/Lawyer is likely to remain relevant and employable for
the foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating
one's skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Advocate/Lawyer in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 26,000 - 39,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 59,500 - 95,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
LAW & LEGAL
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 196 of 1372

— earnings can rise to NPR 237,500 - 403,500 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Advocate/Lawyer-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia): approximately
USD 3,250 - 15,450 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 5,200 - 21,050 / month;
Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 4,300 - 13,900 / month. As these figures show, Gulf countries
generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to zero income
tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and Western Europe)
tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall in between,
combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth remembering that
higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa and relocation costs,
and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Advocate/Lawyer, the core skills generally required include
Legal Knowledge, Argumentation, and Research. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills, most
professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost every role
— even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the
public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep
learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced professionals as being just as
important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree certificate.
Legal Knowledge
Argumentation
Research
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in law & legal, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations or fully
independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Advocate/Lawyer offers a genuinely solid
combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to seek
out an internship, a small real-world project, or direct conversations with working professionals in this
field to get a clearer, first-hand sense of whether this path truly fits your interests and strengths.

074
Legal Advisor/Corporate Lawyer
OVERVIEW
A Legal Advisor/Corporate Lawyer is a professional working within the Law & Legal sector, and in simple
terms, this role means the following: Provides legal advice and guidance to companies. While the title
itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or
people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage.
This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to law & legal because it offers a
fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built during
college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Legal Advisor/Corporate Lawyer typically spends
their time applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to law & legal. Early in a career, most of
the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in this role
usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A high-paying legal role always needed within the corporate sector. Beyond that immediate appeal,
this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose
and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the
country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: As Nepal continues to develop its corporate regulatory framework,
intellectual property law, and human rights institutions, the legal profession is evolving well beyond
traditional courtroom litigation into corporate advisory, compliance, and policy work. Taken together,
these trends suggest that a career as a Legal Advisor/Corporate Lawyer is likely to remain relevant and
employable for the foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive will require
continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL

predictable progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector.
At the entry level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to
earn around NPR 24,500 - 37,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level
professionals in this role tend to earn closer to NPR 56,500 - 90,500 / month. At the senior or specialist
level — including those who have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their

practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or people-facing skills, and a

works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/freelance professional,
but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A fast entry point into the legal field. Beyond that immediate appeal, this career also tends to offer
something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose and visible impact, whether
that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the country as a whole. For students
who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a career like this does not require
having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in this field started with only a
general interest and built specific expertise gradually through coursework, internships, and early job
experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: As Nepal continues to develop its corporate regulatory framework,
intellectual property law, and human rights institutions, the legal profession is evolving well beyond
traditional courtroom litigation into corporate advisory, compliance, and policy work. Taken together,
these trends suggest that a career as a Paralegal is likely to remain relevant and employable for the
foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's
skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Paralegal in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable progression
tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry level, fresh
graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around NPR 21,500
- 32,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role tend to
earn closer to NPR 49,500 - 79,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who have
built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice —
LAW & LEGAL
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 207 of 1372

earnings can rise to NPR 198,000 - 336,500 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Paralegal-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia): approximately USD
2,700 - 12,850 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 4,300 - 17,550 / month;
Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 3,600 - 11,600 / month. As these figures show, Gulf countries
generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to zero income
tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and Western Europe)
tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall in between,
combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth remembering that
higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa and relocation costs,
and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Paralegal, the core skills generally required include
Documentation, Research, and Organization. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills, most
professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost every role
— even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the
public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep
learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced professionals as being just as
important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree certificate.
Documentation
Research
Organization
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in law & legal, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations or fully
independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Paralegal offers a genuinely solid
combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to seek
out an internship, a small real-world project, or direct conversations with working professionals in this
field to get a clearer, first-hand sense of whether this path truly fits your interests and strengths.

079
Graphic Designer
OVERVIEW
A Graphic Designer is a professional working within the Arts, Media & Creative sector, and in simple terms,
this role means the following: Designs visual content and branding for businesses. While the title itself may
sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or people-facing
skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage. This career path
is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to arts, media & creative because it offers a fairly
clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built during college
years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Graphic Designer typically spends their time
applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to arts, media & creative. Early in a
career, most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior
professionals in this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring
junior staff, and sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary
significantly depending on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO,
or as an independent/freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely
consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
One of the most in-demand freelance skills in the creative market. Beyond that immediate appeal,
this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose
and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the
country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Social media platforms, YouTube, and global freelance marketplaces
have completely changed what a creative career looks like in Nepal — it is now entirely possible to build
an audience, a client base, or a media career without ever needing to relocate abroad. Taken together,
these trends suggest that a career as a Graphic Designer is likely to remain relevant and employable for
the foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating
one's skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Graphic Designer in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 16,500 - 25,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 38,500 - 62,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
ARTS, MEDIA & CREATIVE

have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice

applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to arts, media & creative. Early in a
career, most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior
professionals in this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring
junior staff, and sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary
significantly depending on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO,
or as an independent/freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely
consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A powerful medium for telling stories that can shape society. Beyond that immediate appeal, this
career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose and
visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the country
as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a career like
this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in this field
started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through coursework,
internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Social media platforms, YouTube, and global freelance marketplaces
have completely changed what a creative career looks like in Nepal — it is now entirely possible to build
an audience, a client base, or a media career without ever needing to relocate abroad. Taken together,
these trends suggest that a career as a Filmmaker/Director is likely to remain relevant and employable for
the foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating
one's skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Filmmaker/Director in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 16,500 - 24,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 38,500 - 61,500 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
ARTS, MEDIA & CREATIVE
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 217 of 1372

have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
— earnings can rise to NPR 100,500 - 171,000 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Filmmaker/Director-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia):
approximately USD 1,100 - 6,550 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 2,550 -
8,900 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 2,750 - 6,550 / month. As these figures show,
Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to
zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and
Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall
in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Filmmaker/Director, the core skills generally required
include Storytelling, Direction, and Technical Filming. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills, most
professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost every role
— even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the
public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep
learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced professionals as being just as
important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree certificate.
Storytelling
Direction
Technical Filming
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in arts, media & creative, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations
or fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Filmmaker/Director offers a genuinely solid
combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to seek

084
Musician/Music Producer
OVERVIEW
A Musician/Music Producer is a professional working within the Arts, Media & Creative sector, and in
simple terms, this role means the following: Creates and produces original music. While the title itself may
sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or people-facing
skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage. This career path
is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to arts, media & creative because it offers a fairly
clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built during college
years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Musician/Music Producer typically spends their
time applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to arts, media & creative. Early in a
career, most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior
professionals in this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring
junior staff, and sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary
significantly depending on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO,

consistent.

Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Animator in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable progression
tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry level, fresh
graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around NPR 17,500 -

OVERVIEW

means the following: Reports on news and events for the public. While the title itself may sound broad, in
practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or people-facing skills, and a
defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage. This career path is worth
exploring seriously for students who are drawn to arts, media & creative because it offers a fairly clear
