Fashion Designer
OVERVIEW
A Fashion Designer is a professional working within the Arts, Media & Creative sector, and in simple terms,
this role means the following: Designs clothing and fashion collections. While the title itself may sound
broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or people-facing skills,
and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage. This career path is
worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to arts, media & creative because it offers a fairly
clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built during college
years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Fashion Designer typically spends their time
applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to arts, media & creative. Early in a
career, most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior
professionals in this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring
junior staff, and sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary
significantly depending on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO,
or as an independent/freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely
consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A unique career combining creativity with business skills. Beyond that immediate appeal, this career
also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose and visible
impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the country as a
whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a career like this
does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in this field
started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through coursework,
internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Social media platforms, YouTube, and global freelance marketplaces
have completely changed what a creative career looks like in Nepal — it is now entirely possible to build
an audience, a client base, or a media career without ever needing to relocate abroad. Taken together,
these trends suggest that a career as a Fashion Designer is likely to remain relevant and employable for
the foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating
one's skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Fashion Designer in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 17,500 - 26,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 40,500 - 65,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
ARTS, MEDIA & CREATIVE
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 230 of 1372

have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
— earnings can rise to NPR 106,000 - 180,500 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Fashion Designer-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia): approximately
USD 1,150 - 6,900 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 2,700 - 9,400 / month;
Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 2,900 - 6,900 / month. As these figures show, Gulf countries
generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to zero income
tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and Western Europe)
tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall in between,
combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth remembering that
higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa and relocation costs,
and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Fashion Designer, the core skills generally required include
Design, Sewing/Pattern Making, and Trend Awareness. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills, most
professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost every role
— even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the
public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep
learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced professionals as being just as
important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree certificate.
Design
Sewing/Pattern Making
Trend Awareness
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in arts, media & creative, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations
or fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Fashion Designer offers a genuinely solid
combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to seek
out an internship, a small real-world project, or direct conversations with working professionals in this
field to get a clearer, first-hand sense of whether this path truly fits your interests and strengths.
ENTRY
NPR 17,500 - 26,000 / month
MID
NPR 40,500 - 65,000 / month
SENIOR
NPR 106,000 - 180,500 / month
Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia)

Australia & New Zealand

091
Research Scientist
OVERVIEW
A Research Scientist is a professional working within the Science & Research sector, and in simple terms,
this role means the following: Conducts experiments and research to expand scientific knowledge. While
the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical
or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage.
This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to science & research because it
offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built
during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Research Scientist typically spends their time
applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to science & research. Early in a career,
most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in
this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A fundamental career focused on solving the world's biggest problems. Beyond that immediate
appeal, this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear
purpose and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or
the country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Research careers in Nepal are still maturing, with the strongest
opportunities currently found in university-affiliated research centers, international NGOs, and cross-
border academic collaborations, though the overall research funding environment domestically remains
limited compared to more developed economies. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a
Research Scientist is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though as with
any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a
single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Research Scientist in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 23,000 - 34,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 44,000 - 70,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
SCIENCE & RESEARCH

have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
— earnings can rise to NPR 94,000 - 159,500 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,

clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine
willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced

out an internship, a small real-world project, or direct conversations with working professionals in this
field to get a clearer, first-hand sense of whether this path truly fits your interests and strengths.

093
Astronomer/Astrophysicist
OVERVIEW
A Astronomer/Astrophysicist is a professional working within the Science & Research sector, and in simple
terms, this role means the following: Studies stars, planets, and the universe. While the title itself may
sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or people-facing
skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage. This career path
is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to science & research because it offers a fairly
clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built during college
years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Astronomer/Astrophysicist typically spends their
time applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to science & research. Early in a career,
most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in
this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
One of the most fascinating careers for naturally curious students. Beyond that immediate appeal,
this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose
and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the
country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Research careers in Nepal are still maturing, with the strongest
opportunities currently found in university-affiliated research centers, international NGOs, and cross-
border academic collaborations, though the overall research funding environment domestically remains
limited compared to more developed economies. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a
Astronomer/Astrophysicist is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though
as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying
solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Astronomer/Astrophysicist in Nepal generally follows a fairly
predictable progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector.
At the entry level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to
earn around NPR 21,500 - 32,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level
professionals in this role tend to earn closer to NPR 41,000 - 66,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level
SCIENCE & RESEARCH
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 242 of 1372

— including those who have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own
independent practice — earnings can rise to NPR 88,500 - 150,000 / month, though reaching this level
naturally takes time, consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Astronomer/Astrophysicist-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia):
approximately USD 1,750 - 8,300 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 3,750 -
11,750 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 3,550 - 8,400 / month. As these figures show,
Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to
zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and
Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall
in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Astronomer/Astrophysicist, the core skills generally
required include Physics, Math, and Research. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills, most
professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost every role
— even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the
public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep
learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced professionals as being just as
important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree certificate.
Physics
Math
Research
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in science & research, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations or
fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Astronomer/Astrophysicist offers a
genuinely solid combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is
usually to seek out an internship, a small real-world project, or direct conversations with working

ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 253 of 1372

098
Statistician
OVERVIEW
A Statistician is a professional working within the Science & Research sector, and in simple terms, this role
means the following: Collects and analyzes data to extract meaningful insights. While the title itself may
sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or people-facing
skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage. This career path
is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to science & research because it offers a fairly
clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built during college
years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Statistician typically spends their time applying
specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with colleagues, clients,
supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying current with the latest
tools, standards, or best practices relevant to science & research. Early in a career, most of the work tends
to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in this role usually take on
responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and sometimes managing
entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending on whether someone
works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/freelance professional,
but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A versatile analytical career applicable across every industry. Beyond that immediate appeal, this
career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose and
visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the country
as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a career like
this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in this field
started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through coursework,
internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Research careers in Nepal are still maturing, with the strongest
opportunities currently found in university-affiliated research centers, international NGOs, and cross-
border academic collaborations, though the overall research funding environment domestically remains
limited compared to more developed economies. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a
Statistician is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though as with any field,
staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a single
degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Statistician in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable progression
tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry level, fresh
graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around NPR 23,500
- 35,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role tend to
earn closer to NPR 45,000 - 72,500 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who have
SCIENCE & RESEARCH
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 254 of 1372

built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice —
earnings can rise to NPR 97,000 - 164,500 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Statistician-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia): approximately USD
1,950 - 9,100 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 4,100 - 12,850 / month;
Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 3,850 - 9,250 / month. As these figures show, Gulf countries
generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to zero income
tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and Western Europe)
tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall in between,
combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth remembering that
higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa and relocation costs,
and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Statistician, the core skills generally required include
Statistics, Data Analysis, and Math. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills, most professionals who
do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost every role — even highly
technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the public. Time
management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep learning
throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced professionals as being just as
important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree certificate.
Statistics
Data Analysis

position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in science & research, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations or
fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Statistician offers a genuinely solid
combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to seek
out an internship, a small real-world project, or direct conversations with working professionals in this
field to get a clearer, first-hand sense of whether this path truly fits your interests and strengths.

Agricultural Scientist
OVERVIEW
A Agricultural Scientist is a professional working within the Agriculture & Environment sector, and in simple
terms, this role means the following: Researches new techniques and improved crop varieties. While the
title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or
people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage.
This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to agriculture & environment
because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills
are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Agricultural Scientist typically spends their time
applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to agriculture & environment. Early in a
career, most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior
professionals in this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring
junior staff, and sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary
significantly depending on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO,
or as an independent/freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely
consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A core, essential career for agriculture-dependent Nepal. Beyond that immediate appeal, this career
also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose and visible
impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the country as a
whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a career like this
does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in this field
started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through coursework,
internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: As a country where agriculture remains a major part of the economy,
and where climate vulnerability is a growing national concern, Nepal is placing more emphasis on
modern farming techniques, environmental protection, and sustainable resource management, creating
fresh opportunities in this space. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a Agricultural
Scientist is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though as with any field,
staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a single
degree or qualification.

SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Horticulturist, the core skills generally required include
Plant Science, Farming Techniques, and Business. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills, most
professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost every role
— even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the
public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep
learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced professionals as being just as
important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree certificate.

common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in agriculture & environment, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger

103
Environmental Scientist
OVERVIEW
A Environmental Scientist is a professional working within the Agriculture & Environment sector, and in
simple terms, this role means the following: Studies the natural environment and pollution levels. While the
title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or
people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage.
This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to agriculture & environment
because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills
are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Environmental Scientist typically spends their
time applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to agriculture & environment. Early in a
career, most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior
professionals in this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring
junior staff, and sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary
significantly depending on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO,
or as an independent/freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely
consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
Offers global career opportunities tied to sustainability and climate action. Beyond that immediate
appeal, this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear
purpose and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or
the country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: As a country where agriculture remains a major part of the economy,
and where climate vulnerability is a growing national concern, Nepal is placing more emphasis on
modern farming techniques, environmental protection, and sustainable resource management, creating
fresh opportunities in this space. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a Environmental
Scientist is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though as with any field,
staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a single
degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Environmental Scientist in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 20,000 - 30,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
AGRICULTURE & ENVIRONMENT
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 266 of 1372

tend to earn closer to NPR 38,000 - 60,500 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
— earnings can rise to NPR 80,000 - 136,000 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Environmental Scientist-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia):
approximately USD 1,500 - 7,150 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 2,800 -
8,100 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 3,000 - 7,150 / month. As these figures show,
Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to
zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and
Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall
in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Environmental Scientist, the core skills generally required
include Environmental Science, Research, and Field Work. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills,
most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost
every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues,
clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine
willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.
Environmental Science
Research
Field Work

Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in agriculture & environment, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger
organizations or fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-
term option.

SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Renewable Energy Specialist-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia):
approximately USD 1,550 - 7,400 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 2,900 -
8,400 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 3,100 - 7,400 / month. As these figures show,
Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to
zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and
Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall
in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Renewable Energy Specialist, the core skills generally
required include Energy Systems, Engineering Basics, and Sustainability. Beyond these technical or role-
specific skills, most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills,
since almost every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to
colleagues, clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a
genuine willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.
Energy Systems
Engineering Basics
Sustainability
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in agriculture & environment, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger
organizations or fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-
term option.

108
Soil Scientist
OVERVIEW
A Soil Scientist is a professional working within the Agriculture & Environment sector, and in simple terms,
this role means the following: Studies soil quality and fertility for better farming. While the title itself may
sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or people-facing
skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage. This career path
is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to agriculture & environment because it offers a
fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built during
college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Soil Scientist typically spends their time applying
specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with colleagues, clients,
supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying current with the latest
tools, standards, or best practices relevant to agriculture & environment. Early in a career, most of the
work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in this role
usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A fundamental research career that helps boost agricultural output. Beyond that immediate appeal,
this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose
and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the
country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: As a country where agriculture remains a major part of the economy,
and where climate vulnerability is a growing national concern, Nepal is placing more emphasis on
modern farming techniques, environmental protection, and sustainable resource management, creating
fresh opportunities in this space. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a Soil Scientist is
likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying
competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or
qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Soil Scientist in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 18,500 - 27,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 35,000 - 56,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
AGRICULTURE & ENVIRONMENT
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 281 of 1372

have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
— earnings can rise to NPR 73,500 - 125,000 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.

Hotel Manager
OVERVIEW
A Hotel Manager is a professional working within the Hospitality & Tourism sector, and in simple terms, this
role means the following: Manages hotel operations and guest services. While the title itself may sound
broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or people-facing skills,
and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage. This career path is
worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to hospitality & tourism because it offers a fairly
clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built during college

industries, built around trekking, mountaineering, cultural heritage sites, and adventure travel. The sector
experienced a slowdown during the pandemic years but has since rebounded strongly, with visitor
numbers climbing back toward pre-pandemic levels. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career
as a Hotel Manager is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though as with

any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a
single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Event Manager in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 16,500 - 24,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 33,000 - 52,500 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
HOSPITALITY & TOURISM
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 292 of 1372

have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
— earnings can rise to NPR 82,000 - 139,500 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a Event
Manager-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia): approximately USD
1,250 - 6,700 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 2,550 - 7,750 / month;
Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 2,750 - 6,000 / month. As these figures show, Gulf countries
generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to zero income
tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and Western Europe)
tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall in between,
combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth remembering that
higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa and relocation costs,
and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Event Manager, the core skills generally required include
Planning, Coordination, and Problem Solving. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills, most
professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost every role
— even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the
public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep
learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced professionals as being just as
important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree certificate.
Planning
Coordination
Problem Solving
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in hospitality & tourism, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations or
fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Event Manager offers a genuinely solid
combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to seek
out an internship, a small real-world project, or direct conversations with working professionals in this
field to get a clearer, first-hand sense of whether this path truly fits your interests and strengths.

113
Airline Cabin Crew/Pilot
OVERVIEW
A Airline Cabin Crew/Pilot is a professional working within the Hospitality & Tourism sector, and in simple
terms, this role means the following: Manages flights or provides in-flight passenger service. While the title
itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or
people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage.
This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to hospitality & tourism because
it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built
during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Airline Cabin Crew/Pilot typically spends their
time applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to hospitality & tourism. Early in a career,
most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in
this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A glamorous, travel-heavy career for students who love to explore. Beyond that immediate appeal,
this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose
and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the
country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Tourism is one of Nepal's most important foreign-currency-earning
industries, built around trekking, mountaineering, cultural heritage sites, and adventure travel. The sector
experienced a slowdown during the pandemic years but has since rebounded strongly, with visitor
numbers climbing back toward pre-pandemic levels. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career
as a Airline Cabin Crew/Pilot is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though
as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying
solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Airline Cabin Crew/Pilot in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 15,500 - 23,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 31,000 - 49,500 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
HOSPITALITY & TOURISM
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 294 of 1372

have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
— earnings can rise to NPR 77,500 - 132,000 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.

115
Electrician
OVERVIEW
A Electrician is a professional working within the Skilled Trades sector, and in simple terms, this role means
the following: Installs and repairs electrical wiring in homes and buildings. While the title itself may sound
broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or people-facing skills,
and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage. This career path is
worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to skilled trades because it offers a fairly clear route
from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built during college years and
early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Electrician typically spends their time applying
specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with colleagues, clients,
supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying current with the latest
tools, standards, or best practices relevant to skilled trades. Early in a career, most of the work tends to be
more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in this role usually take on
responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and sometimes managing
entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending on whether someone
works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/freelance professional,
but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A practical skill that can lead to independent income quickly. Beyond that immediate appeal, this
career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose and
visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the country
as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a career like
this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in this field
started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through coursework,
internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Skilled trades remain some of the most consistently employable career
paths in Nepal, and demand is arguably even higher abroad — particularly in Gulf countries, Malaysia,
and South Korea, where Nepali tradespeople make up a significant share of the migrant labor workforce.
Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a Electrician is likely to remain relevant and
employable for the foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive will require
continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Electrician in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable progression
tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry level, fresh
graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around NPR 17,000 -
25,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role tend to earn
closer to NPR 34,000 - 54,500 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who have built
strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice — earnings
SKILLED TRADES
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 300 of 1372

can rise to NPR 75,500 - 128,500 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time, consistent skill
development, and often a track record of proven results.

Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Electrician-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia): approximately USD
950 - 5,550 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 3,000 - 11,250 / month;
Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 2,600 - 7,450 / month. As these figures show, Gulf countries
generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to zero income
tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and Western Europe)
tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall in between,
combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth remembering that
higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa and relocation costs,
and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work abroad.

Electrical Systems, Safety, and Troubleshooting. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills, most
professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost every role
— even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the
public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep
learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced professionals as being just as
important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree certificate.

WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
Demand keeps rising alongside Nepal's growing number of vehicles. Beyond that immediate appeal,
this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose
and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the
country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Skilled trades remain some of the most consistently employable career
paths in Nepal, and demand is arguably even higher abroad — particularly in Gulf countries, Malaysia,
and South Korea, where Nepali tradespeople make up a significant share of the migrant labor workforce.
Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a Automobile Mechanic is likely to remain relevant
and employable for the foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive will require
continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Automobile Mechanic in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 18,500 - 28,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 37,000 - 59,500 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
SKILLED TRADES
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 304 of 1372

— earnings can rise to NPR 83,000 - 140,500 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Automobile Mechanic-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia):
approximately USD 1,050 - 6,050 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 3,300 -
12,300 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 2,850 - 8,150 / month. As these figures show,
Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to
zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and
Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall
in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Automobile Mechanic, the core skills generally required
include Mechanical Knowledge, Diagnostics, and Hands-on Skill. Beyond these technical or role-specific
skills, most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost
every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues,
clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine
willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.
Mechanical Knowledge
Diagnostics
Hands-on Skill
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in skilled trades, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations or fully
independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Automobile Mechanic offers a genuinely
solid combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to

118
Carpenter
OVERVIEW
A Carpenter is a professional working within the Skilled Trades sector, and in simple terms, this role means
the following: Builds furniture and structures from wood. While the title itself may sound broad, in practice
it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or people-facing skills, and a defined
career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage. This career path is worth exploring
seriously for students who are drawn to skilled trades because it offers a fairly clear route from education
to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Carpenter typically spends their time applying
specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with colleagues, clients,
supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying current with the latest
tools, standards, or best practices relevant to skilled trades. Early in a career, most of the work tends to be
more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in this role usually take on
responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and sometimes managing
entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending on whether someone
works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/freelance professional,
but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A traditional skilled career blending creativity and craftsmanship. Beyond that immediate appeal,
this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose
and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the
country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Skilled trades remain some of the most consistently employable career
paths in Nepal, and demand is arguably even higher abroad — particularly in Gulf countries, Malaysia,
and South Korea, where Nepali tradespeople make up a significant share of the migrant labor workforce.
Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a Carpenter is likely to remain relevant and
employable for the foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive will require
continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Carpenter in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable progression
tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry level, fresh
graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around NPR 17,500 -
26,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role tend to earn
closer to NPR 35,500 - 56,500 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who have built
strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice — earnings
SKILLED TRADES

can rise to NPR 78,500 - 133,500 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time, consistent skill
development, and often a track record of proven results.

applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to armed forces & security. Early in a
career, most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior
professionals in this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring
junior staff, and sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary
significantly depending on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO,
or as an independent/freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely
consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A respected uniformed career centered on serving the public directly. Beyond that immediate
appeal, this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear
purpose and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or
the country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Careers in Nepal's armed forces and security services are built around
structured entry examinations, physical fitness standards, and a clear rank-based progression system,
offering long-term stability that is difficult to find in most private sector jobs. Taken together, these trends
suggest that a career as a Nepal Police Officer is likely to remain relevant and employable for the
foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's
skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Nepal Police Officer in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 28,500 - 43,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 53,000 - 85,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
ARMED FORCES & SECURITY
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 316 of 1372

have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
— earnings can rise to NPR 112,500 - 191,500 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Nepal Police Officer-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia):
approximately USD 1,550 - 8,000 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 3,400 -
9,950 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 3,400 - 8,000 / month. As these figures show,
Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to
zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and
Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall
in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Nepal Police Officer, the core skills generally required
include Law Knowledge, Physical Fitness, and Integrity. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills, most
professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost every role
— even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the
public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep
learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced professionals as being just as
important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree certificate.
Law Knowledge
Physical Fitness
Integrity
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in armed forces & security, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations
or fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Nepal Police Officer offers a genuinely solid
combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to seek

Armed Police Force (APF) Officer
OVERVIEW
A Armed Police Force (APF) Officer is a professional working within the Armed Forces & Security sector, and
in simple terms, this role means the following: Protects national borders and internal security. While the
title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or
people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage.
This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to armed forces & security
because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills
are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Armed Police Force (APF) Officer typically spends
their time applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to armed forces & security. Early in a
career, most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior
professionals in this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring
junior staff, and sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary
significantly depending on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO,
or as an independent/freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely
consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
Combines adventure with meaningful service to the nation. Beyond that immediate appeal, this
career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose and
visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the country
as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a career like

from cyber threats. While the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific
