progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 15,500 - 23,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 31,000 - 49,500 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
HOSPITALITY & TOURISM
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 925 of 1372

have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
— earnings can rise to NPR 77,000 - 131,000 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Bartender/Mixologist-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia):
approximately USD 1,150 - 6,250 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 2,400 -
7,250 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 2,600 - 5,650 / month. As these figures show,
Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to
zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and
Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall
in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Bartender/Mixologist, the core skills generally required
include Mixology, Customer Service, and Creativity. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills, most
professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost every role
— even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the
public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep
learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced professionals as being just as
important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree certificate.
Mixology
Customer Service
Creativity
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in hospitality & tourism, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations or
fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Bartender/Mixologist offers a genuinely
solid combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to

350
Tour Company Operations Manager
OVERVIEW
A Tour Company Operations Manager is a professional working within the Hospitality & Tourism sector,
and in simple terms, this role means the following: Manages logistics and operations for a tourism
company. While the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily
tasks, technical or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around
from an early stage. This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to hospitality
& tourism because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right
foundational skills are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Tour Company Operations Manager typically
spends their time applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field,
coordinating with colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job,
and staying current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to hospitality & tourism.
Early in a career, most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior
professionals in this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring
junior staff, and sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary
significantly depending on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO,
or as an independent/freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely
consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A behind-the-scenes leadership role in Nepal's booming adventure tourism industry. Beyond that
immediate appeal, this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense
of clear purpose and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities,
companies, or the country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth
noting that a career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many
professionals in this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually
through coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Tourism is one of Nepal's most important foreign-currency-earning
industries, built around trekking, mountaineering, cultural heritage sites, and adventure travel. The sector
experienced a slowdown during the pandemic years but has since rebounded strongly, with visitor
numbers climbing back toward pre-pandemic levels. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career
as a Tour Company Operations Manager is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable
future, though as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather
than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Tour Company Operations Manager in Nepal generally follows a fairly
predictable progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector.
At the entry level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to
earn around NPR 16,000 - 24,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level
HOSPITALITY & TOURISM

professionals in this role tend to earn closer to NPR 32,500 - 51,500 / month. At the senior or specialist level
— including those who have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own
independent practice — earnings can rise to NPR 81,000 - 137,500 / month, though reaching this level
naturally takes time, consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.

colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to hospitality & tourism. Early in a career,
most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in
this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
Helps rural Nepali communities directly benefit from tourism income. Beyond that immediate
appeal, this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear
purpose and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or
the country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Tourism is one of Nepal's most important foreign-currency-earning
industries, built around trekking, mountaineering, cultural heritage sites, and adventure travel. The sector
experienced a slowdown during the pandemic years but has since rebounded strongly, with visitor
numbers climbing back toward pre-pandemic levels. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career
as a Homestay Tourism Coordinator is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future,
though as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than
relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Homestay Tourism Coordinator in Nepal generally follows a fairly
predictable progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector.
At the entry level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to
earn around NPR 15,000 - 22,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level
professionals in this role tend to earn closer to NPR 30,000 - 47,500 / month. At the senior or specialist level
HOSPITALITY & TOURISM
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 940 of 1372

— including those who have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own
independent practice — earnings can rise to NPR 74,500 - 126,500 / month, though reaching this level
naturally takes time, consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Homestay Tourism Coordinator-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia):
approximately USD 1,100 - 6,050 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 2,350 -
7,000 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 2,500 - 5,450 / month. As these figures show,
Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to
zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and
Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall
in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Homestay Tourism Coordinator, the core skills generally
required include Community Coordination, Hospitality, and Local Knowledge. Beyond these technical or
role-specific skills, most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills,
since almost every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to
colleagues, clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a
genuine willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.
Community Coordination
Hospitality
Local Knowledge
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in hospitality & tourism, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations or
fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Homestay Tourism Coordinator offers a
genuinely solid combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is

Heavy Equipment Operator
OVERVIEW
A Heavy Equipment Operator is a professional working within the Skilled Trades sector, and in simple
terms, this role means the following: Operates cranes, excavators, and other construction machinery.
While the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks,
technical or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an
early stage. This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to skilled trades
because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills
are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Heavy Equipment Operator typically spends their
time applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to skilled trades. Early in a career, most
of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in this role
usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A well-paid trade essential to every major construction project. Beyond that immediate appeal, this
career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose and
visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the country
as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a career like
this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in this field
started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through coursework,
internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Skilled trades remain some of the most consistently employable career
paths in Nepal, and demand is arguably even higher abroad — particularly in Gulf countries, Malaysia,
and South Korea, where Nepali tradespeople make up a significant share of the migrant labor workforce.
Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a Heavy Equipment Operator is likely to remain
relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive will
require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Heavy Equipment Operator in Nepal generally follows a fairly
predictable progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector.
At the entry level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to
earn around NPR 18,000 - 27,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level
professionals in this role tend to earn closer to NPR 36,500 - 58,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level
— including those who have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own
SKILLED TRADES

independent practice — earnings can rise to NPR 81,000 - 137,500 / month, though reaching this level

USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe

359
Roofer
OVERVIEW
A Roofer is a professional working within the Skilled Trades sector, and in simple terms, this role means the
following: Installs and repairs roofing systems on buildings. While the title itself may sound broad, in
practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or people-facing skills, and a
defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage. This career path is worth
exploring seriously for students who are drawn to skilled trades because it offers a fairly clear route from
education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built during college years and early
internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Roofer typically spends their time applying
specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with colleagues, clients,
supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying current with the latest
tools, standards, or best practices relevant to skilled trades. Early in a career, most of the work tends to be
more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in this role usually take on
responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and sometimes managing
entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending on whether someone
works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/freelance professional,
but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A steady construction trade always in demand. Beyond that immediate appeal, this career also
tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose and visible
impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the country as a
whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a career like this
does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in this field
started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through coursework,
internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Skilled trades remain some of the most consistently employable career
paths in Nepal, and demand is arguably even higher abroad — particularly in Gulf countries, Malaysia,
and South Korea, where Nepali tradespeople make up a significant share of the migrant labor workforce.
Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a Roofer is likely to remain relevant and employable
for the foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating
one's skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Roofer in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable progression tied
to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry level, fresh
graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around NPR 16,000
- 24,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role tend to
earn closer to NPR 32,500 - 52,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who have
built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice —
SKILLED TRADES
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 952 of 1372

earnings can rise to NPR 72,000 - 122,500 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Roofer-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia): approximately USD 900 -
5,300 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 2,900 - 10,750 / month; Australia &
New Zealand: approximately USD 2,500 - 7,100 / month. As these figures show, Gulf countries generally
offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to zero income tax and
employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and Western Europe) tend to
offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall in between, combining
strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth remembering that higher foreign
salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa and relocation costs, and time
away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Roofer, the core skills generally required include Roofing
Techniques, Safety, and Physical Fitness. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills, most professionals
who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost every role — even highly
technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the public. Time
management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep learning
throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced professionals as being just as
important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree certificate.
Roofing Techniques
Safety
Physical Fitness
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in skilled trades, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations or fully
independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Roofer offers a genuinely solid
combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to seek
out an internship, a small real-world project, or direct conversations with working professionals in this
field to get a clearer, first-hand sense of whether this path truly fits your interests and strengths.
ENTRY
NPR 16,000 - 24,500 / month
MID
NPR 32,500 - 52,000 / month
SENIOR
NPR 72,000 - 122,500 / month
Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia)

position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in skilled trades, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations or fully
independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Sign Maker/Fabricator offers a genuinely
solid combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to
seek out an internship, a small real-world project, or direct conversations with working professionals in
this field to get a clearer, first-hand sense of whether this path truly fits your interests and strengths.

364
Elevator Technician
OVERVIEW
A Elevator Technician is a professional working within the Skilled Trades sector, and in simple terms, this
role means the following: Installs and maintains elevators and escalators. While the title itself may sound
broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or people-facing skills,
and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage. This career path is
worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to skilled trades because it offers a fairly clear route
from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built during college years and
early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Elevator Technician typically spends their time
applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to skilled trades. Early in a career, most
of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in this role
usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
One of the highest-paying skilled trades internationally. Beyond that immediate appeal, this career
also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose and visible
impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the country as a
whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a career like this
does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in this field
started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through coursework,
internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Skilled trades remain some of the most consistently employable career
paths in Nepal, and demand is arguably even higher abroad — particularly in Gulf countries, Malaysia,
and South Korea, where Nepali tradespeople make up a significant share of the migrant labor workforce.
Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a Elevator Technician is likely to remain relevant
and employable for the foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive will require
continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Elevator Technician in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 19,000 - 28,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 38,000 - 61,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
SKILLED TRADES
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 964 of 1372

— earnings can rise to NPR 85,000 - 144,000 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Elevator Technician-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia):
approximately USD 1,050 - 6,200 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 3,400 -
12,600 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 2,900 - 8,350 / month. As these figures show,
Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to
zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and
Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall
in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Elevator Technician, the core skills generally required
include Mechanical Skill, Electrical Knowledge, and Safety. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills,
most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost
every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues,
clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine
willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.
Mechanical Skill
Electrical Knowledge
Safety

and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Stone Mason/Sculptor, the core skills generally required
include Stone Working, Precision, and Creativity. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills, most
professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost every role
— even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the
public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep
learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced professionals as being just as
important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree certificate.
Stone Working
Precision
Creativity
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in skilled trades, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations or fully
independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Stone Mason/Sculptor offers a genuinely
solid combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to
seek out an internship, a small real-world project, or direct conversations with working professionals in
this field to get a clearer, first-hand sense of whether this path truly fits your interests and strengths.

369
Landscaper/Groundskeeper
OVERVIEW
A Landscaper/Groundskeeper is a professional working within the Skilled Trades sector, and in simple
terms, this role means the following: Designs and maintains outdoor gardens and green spaces. While the
title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or
people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage.
This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to skilled trades because it offers
a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built during
college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Landscaper/Groundskeeper typically spends
their time applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to skilled trades. Early in a career, most
of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in this role
usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A steady, outdoor-friendly trade growing with urban development. Beyond that immediate appeal,
this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose
and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the
country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Skilled trades remain some of the most consistently employable career
paths in Nepal, and demand is arguably even higher abroad — particularly in Gulf countries, Malaysia,
and South Korea, where Nepali tradespeople make up a significant share of the migrant labor workforce.
Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a Landscaper/Groundskeeper is likely to remain
relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive will
require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Landscaper/Groundskeeper in Nepal generally follows a fairly
predictable progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector.
At the entry level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to
earn around NPR 20,500 - 31,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level
professionals in this role tend to earn closer to NPR 41,000 - 66,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level
— including those who have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own
SKILLED TRADES
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 976 of 1372

independent practice — earnings can rise to NPR 91,500 - 155,500 / month, though reaching this level
naturally takes time, consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Landscaper/Groundskeeper-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia):
approximately USD 1,150 - 6,700 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 3,650 -
13,600 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 3,150 - 9,000 / month. As these figures show,
Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to
zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and
Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall
in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Landscaper/Groundskeeper, the core skills generally
required include Plant Knowledge, Physical Work, and Design Sense. Beyond these technical or role-
specific skills, most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills,
since almost every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to
colleagues, clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a
genuine willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.

Physical Work

Intelligence Officer
OVERVIEW
A Intelligence Officer is a professional working within the Armed Forces & Security sector, and in simple
terms, this role means the following: Gathers and analyzes information for national security purposes.
While the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks,
technical or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an
early stage. This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to armed forces &
security because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right
foundational skills are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Intelligence Officer typically spends their time
applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to armed forces & security. Early in a

structured entry examinations, physical fitness standards, and a clear rank-based progression system,
offering long-term stability that is difficult to find in most private sector jobs. Taken together, these trends
suggest that a career as a Intelligence Officer is likely to remain relevant and employable for the
foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's
skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.

tend to earn closer to NPR 47,000 - 75,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who

own independent practice — earnings can rise to NPR 125,000 - 212,500 / month, though reaching this level
naturally takes time, consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Bomb Disposal/EOD Specialist-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia):
approximately USD 1,700 - 8,850 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 3,750 -
11,100 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 3,750 - 8,850 / month. As these figures show,
Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to
zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and
Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall
in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Bomb Disposal/EOD Specialist, the core skills generally
required include Technical Precision, Courage, and Training. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills,
most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost
every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues,
clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine
willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.
Technical Precision
Courage
Training
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in armed forces & security, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations
or fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Bomb Disposal/EOD Specialist offers a
genuinely solid combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is

374
Peacekeeping Forces Officer
OVERVIEW
A Peacekeeping Forces Officer is a professional working within the Armed Forces & Security sector, and in
simple terms, this role means the following: Serves in UN peacekeeping missions abroad. While the title
itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or
people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage.
This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to armed forces & security
because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills
are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Peacekeeping Forces Officer typically spends
their time applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to armed forces & security. Early in a
career, most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior
professionals in this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring
junior staff, and sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary
significantly depending on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO,
or as an independent/freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely
consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
Nepal is one of the world's largest UN peacekeeping contributors — a source of pride and income.
Beyond that immediate appeal, this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely
value: a sense of clear purpose and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients,
communities, companies, or the country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different
options, it is worth noting that a career like this does not require having everything figured out
immediately — many professionals in this field started with only a general interest and built specific
expertise gradually through coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Careers in Nepal's armed forces and security services are built around
structured entry examinations, physical fitness standards, and a clear rank-based progression system,
offering long-term stability that is difficult to find in most private sector jobs. Taken together, these trends
suggest that a career as a Peacekeeping Forces Officer is likely to remain relevant and employable for the
foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's
skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Peacekeeping Forces Officer in Nepal generally follows a fairly
predictable progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector.
At the entry level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to
earn around NPR 25,500 - 38,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level
professionals in this role tend to earn closer to NPR 47,000 - 75,500 / month. At the senior or specialist level
ARMED FORCES & SECURITY

— including those who have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own

this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Careers in Nepal's armed forces and security services are built around
structured entry examinations, physical fitness standards, and a clear rank-based progression system,
offering long-term stability that is difficult to find in most private sector jobs. Taken together, these trends
suggest that a career as a Risk Management Consultant is likely to remain relevant and employable for
the foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating
one's skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Risk Management Consultant in Nepal generally follows a fairly
predictable progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector.
At the entry level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to
earn around NPR 27,500 - 41,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level
professionals in this role tend to earn closer to NPR 51,500 - 82,500 / month. At the senior or specialist level
ARMED FORCES & SECURITY
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 1003 of 1372

— including those who have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own
independent practice — earnings can rise to NPR 109,000 - 185,500 / month, though reaching this level
naturally takes time, consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a Risk
Management Consultant-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia):
approximately USD 1,500 - 7,750 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 3,250 -
9,650 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 3,250 - 7,750 / month. As these figures show,
Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to
zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and
Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall
in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Risk Management Consultant, the core skills generally
required include Risk Analysis, Consulting, and Communication. Beyond these technical or role-specific
skills, most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost
every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues,
clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine
willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.
Risk Analysis
Consulting
Communication

position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in armed forces & security, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations
or fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.

genuinely solid combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is

Emergency Dispatcher
OVERVIEW
A Emergency Dispatcher is a professional working within the Armed Forces & Security sector, and in simple
terms, this role means the following: Coordinates emergency response calls and resources. While the title
itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or
people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage.
This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to armed forces & security
because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills
are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Emergency Dispatcher typically spends their time
applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to armed forces & security. Early in a
career, most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior
professionals in this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring
junior staff, and sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary
significantly depending on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO,
or as an independent/freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely
consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A high-pressure, life-saving behind-the-scenes security career. Beyond that immediate appeal, this
career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose and
visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the country
as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a career like
this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in this field
started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through coursework,
internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Careers in Nepal's armed forces and security services are built around
structured entry examinations, physical fitness standards, and a clear rank-based progression system,
offering long-term stability that is difficult to find in most private sector jobs. Taken together, these trends
suggest that a career as a Emergency Dispatcher is likely to remain relevant and employable for the
foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's
skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Emergency Dispatcher in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 30,500 - 46,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 57,000 - 91,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
ARMED FORCES & SECURITY

have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
— earnings can rise to NPR 120,500 - 204,500 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,

every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues,

Cricket Player (Professional)
OVERVIEW
A Cricket Player (Professional) is a professional working within the Sports sector, and in simple terms, this
role means the following: Competes professionally in domestic or international cricket. While the title itself
may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or people-
facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage. This
career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to sports because it offers a fairly
clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built during college
years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Cricket Player (Professional) typically spends
their time applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to sports. Early in a career, most of the
work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in this role
usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
Nepal's cricket team's rising global profile is opening real professional pathways. Beyond that
immediate appeal, this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense
of clear purpose and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities,
companies, or the country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth
noting that a career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many
professionals in this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually
through coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Nepal's sports industry is still developing compared to global standards,
but growing sponsorship interest, improved media coverage, and rising youth participation in cricket,
football, and other sports are gradually creating more viable professional pathways. Taken together,
these trends suggest that a career as a Cricket Player (Professional) is likely to remain relevant and
employable for the foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive will require
continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Cricket Player (Professional) in Nepal generally follows a fairly
predictable progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector.
At the entry level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to
earn around NPR 12,500 - 19,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level
professionals in this role tend to earn closer to NPR 36,500 - 58,500 / month. At the senior or specialist level
— including those who have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own

independent practice — earnings can rise to NPR 94,000 - 159,500 / month, though reaching this level

applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to sports. Early in a career, most of the
work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in this role
usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A respected, discipline-building career with strong local demand. Beyond that immediate appeal,
this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose
and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the
country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Nepal's sports industry is still developing compared to global standards,
but growing sponsorship interest, improved media coverage, and rising youth participation in cricket,
football, and other sports are gradually creating more viable professional pathways. Taken together,
these trends suggest that a career as a Martial Arts Instructor is likely to remain relevant and employable
for the foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating
one's skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Martial Arts Instructor in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 13,500 - 20,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 40,000 - 63,500 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
SPORTS
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 1017 of 1372

— earnings can rise to NPR 102,500 - 174,000 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Martial Arts Instructor-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia):
approximately USD 1,150 - 10,350 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 2,850 -
14,800 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 2,850 - 10,350 / month. As these figures show,
Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to
zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and
Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall
in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Martial Arts Instructor, the core skills generally required
include Martial Arts Skill, Teaching, and Discipline. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills, most
professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost every role
— even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the
public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep
learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced professionals as being just as
important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree certificate.
Martial Arts Skill
Teaching
Discipline
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in sports, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations or fully
independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Martial Arts Instructor offers a genuinely
solid combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to
seek out an internship, a small real-world project, or direct conversations with working professionals in
this field to get a clearer, first-hand sense of whether this path truly fits your interests and strengths.

Mountaineer/Professional Climber
OVERVIEW
A Mountaineer/Professional Climber is a professional working within the Sports sector, and in simple
terms, this role means the following: Climbs peaks professionally, often including Everest expeditions.
While the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks,
technical or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an
early stage. This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to sports because it
offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built
during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Mountaineer/Professional Climber typically
spends their time applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field,
coordinating with colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job,
and staying current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to sports. Early in a career,
most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in
this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
One of the most globally recognized career paths a Nepali athlete can pursue. Beyond that
immediate appeal, this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense
of clear purpose and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities,
companies, or the country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth
noting that a career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many
professionals in this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually
through coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Nepal's sports industry is still developing compared to global standards,
but growing sponsorship interest, improved media coverage, and rising youth participation in cricket,
football, and other sports are gradually creating more viable professional pathways. Taken together,
these trends suggest that a career as a Mountaineer/Professional Climber is likely to remain relevant and
employable for the foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive will require
continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.

Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Mountaineer/Professional Climber in Nepal generally follows a fairly
predictable progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector.
At the entry level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to
earn around NPR 11,500 - 17,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals
in this role tend to earn closer to NPR 34,000 - 54,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including
those who have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent
SPORTS

practice — earnings can rise to NPR 87,000 - 148,000 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes
time, consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.

itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or
people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage.
This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to sports because it offers a fairly
clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built during college
years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Sports Marketing Manager typically spends their
time applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to sports. Early in a career, most of the
work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in this role
usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
Combines love of sports with a dynamic marketing career. Beyond that immediate appeal, this
career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose and
visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the country
as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a career like
this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in this field
started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through coursework,
internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Nepal's sports industry is still developing compared to global standards,
but growing sponsorship interest, improved media coverage, and rising youth participation in cricket,
football, and other sports are gradually creating more viable professional pathways. Taken together,
these trends suggest that a career as a Sports Marketing Manager is likely to remain relevant and
employable for the foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive will require
continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Sports Marketing Manager in Nepal generally follows a fairly
predictable progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector.
At the entry level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to
earn around NPR 13,500 - 20,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level
professionals in this role tend to earn closer to NPR 39,500 - 63,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level
— including those who have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own
SPORTS
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 1029 of 1372

independent practice — earnings can rise to NPR 101,000 - 172,000 / month, though reaching this level
naturally takes time, consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Sports Marketing Manager-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia):
approximately USD 1,100 - 10,200 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 2,800 -
14,600 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 2,800 - 10,200 / month. As these figures show,
Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to
zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and
Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall
in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Sports Marketing Manager, the core skills generally
required include Marketing, Negotiation, and Sports Knowledge. Beyond these technical or role-specific
skills, most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost
every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues,
clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine
willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.
Marketing
Negotiation
Sports Knowledge
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in sports, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations or fully
independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Sports Marketing Manager offers a
genuinely solid combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is
usually to seek out an internship, a small real-world project, or direct conversations with working

Production Manager
OVERVIEW
A Production Manager is a professional working within the Manufacturing & Production sector, and in
simple terms, this role means the following: Oversees daily manufacturing operations and output targets.
While the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks,
technical or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an
early stage. This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to manufacturing &
production because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right
foundational skills are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Production Manager typically spends their time
applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to manufacturing & production. Early in
a career, most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior
professionals in this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring
junior staff, and sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary
significantly depending on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO,
or as an independent/freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely
consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A leadership career central to every factory's success. Beyond that immediate appeal, this career
also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose and visible
impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the country as a
whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a career like this
does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in this field
started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through coursework,
internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Nepal's manufacturing base is smaller than its neighbors, but growth in
garments, food processing, and light industry is steadily creating demand for production and quality-
focused professionals, particularly in the Kathmandu Valley and Birgunj industrial corridors. Taken
together, these trends suggest that a career as a Production Manager is likely to remain relevant and
employable for the foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive will require
continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Production Manager in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 20,500 - 30,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 40,500 - 65,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who

OVERVIEW

simple terms, this role means the following: Supervises workers and processes on a factory production
line. While the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks,
technical or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an
early stage. This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to manufacturing &
