393
Textile/Garment Production Manager
OVERVIEW
A Textile/Garment Production Manager is a professional working within the Manufacturing & Production
sector, and in simple terms, this role means the following: Manages garment factory production and
quality. While the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily
tasks, technical or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around
from an early stage. This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to
manufacturing & production because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment,
provided the right foundational skills are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Textile/Garment Production Manager typically
spends their time applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field,
coordinating with colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job,
and staying current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to manufacturing &
production. Early in a career, most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while
more senior professionals in this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making,
mentoring junior staff, and sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can
vary significantly depending on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an
NGO, or as an independent/freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains
largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
Directly tied to Nepal's growing textile and garment export sector. Beyond that immediate appeal,
this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose
and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the
country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Nepal's manufacturing base is smaller than its neighbors, but growth in
garments, food processing, and light industry is steadily creating demand for production and quality-
focused professionals, particularly in the Kathmandu Valley and Birgunj industrial corridors. Taken
together, these trends suggest that a career as a Textile/Garment Production Manager is likely to remain
relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive will
require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Textile/Garment Production Manager in Nepal generally follows a
fairly predictable progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this
sector. At the entry level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically
expect to earn around NPR 21,000 - 31,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level
professionals in this role tend to earn closer to NPR 42,000 - 67,500 / month. At the senior or specialist level
MANUFACTURING & PRODUCTION
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 1044 of 1372

— including those who have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own
independent practice — earnings can rise to NPR 94,500 - 160,500 / month, though reaching this level
naturally takes time, consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Textile/Garment Production Manager-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi
Arabia): approximately USD 1,550 - 7,950 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD
3,200 - 10,100 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 3,350 - 8,400 / month. As these figures
show, Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad
thanks to zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK,
and Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically
fall in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Textile/Garment Production Manager, the core skills
generally required include Production Management, Quality Control, and Leadership. Beyond these
technical or role-specific skills, most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong
communication skills, since almost every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires
explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools
or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently
cited by experienced professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed
on a degree certificate.
Production Management
Quality Control
Leadership
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in manufacturing & production, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger
organizations or fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-
term option.
ENTRY
NPR 21,000 - 31,500 / month
MID
NPR 42,000 - 67,500 / month
SENIOR
NPR 94,500 - 160,500 / month
Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia)

USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe

Australia & New Zealand
USD 3,350 - 8,400 / month

398
Food Production Manager
OVERVIEW
A Food Production Manager is a professional working within the Manufacturing & Production sector, and in
simple terms, this role means the following: Oversees manufacturing operations in food processing plants.
While the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks,
technical or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an
early stage. This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to manufacturing &
production because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right
foundational skills are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Food Production Manager typically spends their
time applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to manufacturing & production. Early in
a career, most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior
professionals in this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring
junior staff, and sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary
significantly depending on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO,
or as an independent/freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely
consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
Directly tied to Nepal's growing packaged food industry. Beyond that immediate appeal, this career
also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose and visible
impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the country as a
whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a career like this
does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in this field
started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through coursework,
internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Nepal's manufacturing base is smaller than its neighbors, but growth in
garments, food processing, and light industry is steadily creating demand for production and quality-
focused professionals, particularly in the Kathmandu Valley and Birgunj industrial corridors. Taken
together, these trends suggest that a career as a Food Production Manager is likely to remain relevant
and employable for the foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive will require
continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Food Production Manager in Nepal generally follows a fairly
predictable progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector.
At the entry level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to
earn around NPR 21,000 - 32,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level
professionals in this role tend to earn closer to NPR 42,500 - 68,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level
MANUFACTURING & PRODUCTION
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 1059 of 1372

— including those who have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own
independent practice — earnings can rise to NPR 95,000 - 161,500 / month, though reaching this level
naturally takes time, consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a Food
Production Manager-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia):
approximately USD 1,550 - 8,000 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 3,250 -
10,200 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 3,350 - 8,450 / month. As these figures show,
Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to
zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and
Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall
in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Food Production Manager, the core skills generally
required include Food Safety, Production Management, and Quality Control. Beyond these technical or
role-specific skills, most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills,
since almost every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to
colleagues, clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a
genuine willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.
Food Safety
Production Management
Quality Control

position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in manufacturing & production, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger
organizations or fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-
term option.

Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to
zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and
Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall
in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Assembly Line Technician, the core skills generally required
include Precision, Speed, and Teamwork. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills, most professionals
who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost every role — even highly
technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the public. Time
management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep learning
throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced professionals as being just as
important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree certificate.
Precision
Speed
Teamwork
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in manufacturing & production, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger
organizations or fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-
term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Assembly Line Technician offers a
genuinely solid combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is

403
Industrial Maintenance Technician
OVERVIEW
A Industrial Maintenance Technician is a professional working within the Manufacturing & Production
sector, and in simple terms, this role means the following: Repairs and maintains factory machinery and
equipment. While the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of
daily tasks, technical or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan
around from an early stage. This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to
manufacturing & production because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment,
provided the right foundational skills are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Industrial Maintenance Technician typically
spends their time applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field,
coordinating with colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job,
and staying current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to manufacturing &
production. Early in a career, most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while
more senior professionals in this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making,
mentoring junior staff, and sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can
vary significantly depending on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an
NGO, or as an independent/freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains
largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A well-paid, always-needed technical trade in every factory. Beyond that immediate appeal, this
career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose and
visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the country
as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a career like
this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in this field
started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through coursework,
internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Nepal's manufacturing base is smaller than its neighbors, but growth in
garments, food processing, and light industry is steadily creating demand for production and quality-
focused professionals, particularly in the Kathmandu Valley and Birgunj industrial corridors. Taken
together, these trends suggest that a career as a Industrial Maintenance Technician is likely to remain
relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive will
require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Industrial Maintenance Technician in Nepal generally follows a fairly
predictable progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector.
At the entry level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to
earn around NPR 18,000 - 27,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level
professionals in this role tend to earn closer to NPR 36,500 - 58,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level
MANUFACTURING & PRODUCTION
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 1074 of 1372

— including those who have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own
independent practice — earnings can rise to NPR 81,500 - 138,500 / month, though reaching this level
naturally takes time, consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Industrial Maintenance Technician-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi
Arabia): approximately USD 1,350 - 6,850 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD
2,800 - 8,700 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 2,850 - 7,200 / month. As these figures
show, Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad
thanks to zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK,
and Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically
fall in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Industrial Maintenance Technician, the core skills generally
required include Mechanical Skill, Troubleshooting, and Safety. Beyond these technical or role-specific
skills, most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost
every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues,
clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine
willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.
Mechanical Skill
Troubleshooting
Safety
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in manufacturing & production, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger
organizations or fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-
term option.

require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Automation Engineer (Manufacturing) in Nepal generally follows a
fairly predictable progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this
sector. At the entry level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically
expect to earn around NPR 17,500 - 26,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level
professionals in this role tend to earn closer to NPR 35,000 - 55,500 / month. At the senior or specialist level
MANUFACTURING & PRODUCTION
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 1086 of 1372

— including those who have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own
independent practice — earnings can rise to NPR 78,000 - 132,500 / month, though reaching this level
naturally takes time, consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Automation Engineer (Manufacturing)-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi
Arabia): approximately USD 1,300 - 6,550 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD
2,650 - 8,350 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 2,750 - 6,900 / month. As these figures
show, Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad
thanks to zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK,
and Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically
fall in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Automation Engineer (Manufacturing), the core skills
generally required include Automation Systems, Engineering, and Problem Solving. Beyond these
technical or role-specific skills, most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong
communication skills, since almost every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires
explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools
or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently
cited by experienced professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed
on a degree certificate.
Automation Systems
Engineering
Problem Solving
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in manufacturing & production, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger
organizations or fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-
term option.

408
Logistics Manager
OVERVIEW
A Logistics Manager is a professional working within the Transportation & Logistics sector, and in simple
terms, this role means the following: Manages the movement and storage of goods for a company. While
the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical
or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage.
This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to transportation & logistics
because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills
are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Logistics Manager typically spends their time
applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to transportation & logistics. Early in a
career, most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior
professionals in this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring
junior staff, and sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary
significantly depending on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO,
or as an independent/freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely
consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A critical growth career as Nepal's e-commerce and trade expand. Beyond that immediate appeal,
this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose
and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the
country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: The rapid rise of e-commerce, food delivery, and cross-border trade is
transforming Nepal's transportation and logistics sector from a purely informal industry into one with real
career structure, professional standards, and growth opportunities. Taken together, these trends suggest
that a career as a Logistics Manager is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable
future, though as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather
than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Logistics Manager in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 19,000 - 28,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 38,500 - 61,500 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
TRANSPORTATION & LOGISTICS

have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
— earnings can rise to NPR 85,500 - 146,000 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,

significantly depending on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO,
or as an independent/freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely
consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A specialized, well-paid niche within Nepal's international trade. Beyond that immediate appeal, this
career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose and
visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the country
as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a career like
this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in this field
started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through coursework,
internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: The rapid rise of e-commerce, food delivery, and cross-border trade is
transforming Nepal's transportation and logistics sector from a purely informal industry into one with real
career structure, professional standards, and growth opportunities. Taken together, these trends suggest
that a career as a Customs Broker is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future,
though as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than
relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Customs Broker in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 19,000 - 28,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 38,000 - 60,500 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
TRANSPORTATION & LOGISTICS
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 1101 of 1372

have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
— earnings can rise to NPR 84,500 - 144,000 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Customs Broker-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia): approximately
USD 1,200 - 6,750 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 3,200 - 10,750 / month;
Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 3,300 - 7,750 / month. As these figures show, Gulf countries
generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to zero income
tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and Western Europe)
tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall in between,
combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth remembering that
higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa and relocation costs,
and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Customs Broker, the core skills generally required include
Customs Regulations, Attention to Detail, and Negotiation. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills,
most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost
every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues,
clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine
willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.
Customs Regulations
Attention to Detail
Negotiation
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in transportation & logistics, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger
organizations or fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-
term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Customs Broker offers a genuinely solid
combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to seek

Fleet Manager
OVERVIEW
A Fleet Manager is a professional working within the Transportation & Logistics sector, and in simple terms,
this role means the following: Manages a company's vehicle fleet operations and maintenance. While the
title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or
people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage.
This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to transportation & logistics
because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills
are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Fleet Manager typically spends their time
applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to transportation & logistics. Early in a
career, most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior
professionals in this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring
junior staff, and sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary
significantly depending on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO,
or as an independent/freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely
consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A practical leadership role within transportation-heavy businesses. Beyond that immediate appeal,
this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose
and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the
country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.

transforming Nepal's transportation and logistics sector from a purely informal industry into one with real
career structure, professional standards, and growth opportunities. Taken together, these trends suggest
that a career as a Fleet Manager is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future,
though as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than
relying solely on a single degree or qualification.

NPR 21,500 - 32,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 43,000 - 68,500 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who

A Public Transport Operations Manager is a professional working within the Transportation & Logistics
sector, and in simple terms, this role means the following: Manages bus or public transit system
operations. While the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of
daily tasks, technical or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan
around from an early stage. This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to
transportation & logistics because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided
the right foundational skills are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Public Transport Operations Manager typically
spends their time applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field,
coordinating with colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job,
and staying current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to transportation & logistics.
Early in a career, most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior
professionals in this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring
junior staff, and sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary
significantly depending on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO,
or as an independent/freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely
consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A public-service career directly improving daily commuting for citizens. Beyond that immediate
appeal, this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear
purpose and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or
the country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: The rapid rise of e-commerce, food delivery, and cross-border trade is
transforming Nepal's transportation and logistics sector from a purely informal industry into one with real
career structure, professional standards, and growth opportunities. Taken together, these trends suggest
that a career as a Public Transport Operations Manager is likely to remain relevant and employable for
the foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating
one's skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Public Transport Operations Manager in Nepal generally follows a
fairly predictable progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this
sector. At the entry level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically
expect to earn around NPR 22,000 - 32,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level
professionals in this role tend to earn closer to NPR 43,500 - 70,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level
TRANSPORTATION & LOGISTICS
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 1116 of 1372

— including those who have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own
independent practice — earnings can rise to NPR 97,500 - 166,000 / month, though reaching this level
naturally takes time, consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Public Transport Operations Manager-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi
Arabia): approximately USD 1,400 - 7,750 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD
3,700 - 12,400 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 3,800 - 8,950 / month. As these
figures show, Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving
abroad thanks to zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA,
Canada, UK, and Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New
Zealand typically fall in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's
also worth remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of
living, visa and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to
work abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Public Transport Operations Manager, the core skills
generally required include Operations Management, Scheduling, and Public Service. Beyond these
technical or role-specific skills, most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong
communication skills, since almost every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires
explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools
or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently
cited by experienced professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed
on a degree certificate.
Operations Management
Scheduling
Public Service
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in transportation & logistics, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger
organizations or fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-
term option.

For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Public Transport Operations Manager
offers a genuinely solid combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next

interests and strengths.

422
Moving/Relocation Services Manager
OVERVIEW
A Moving/Relocation Services Manager is a professional working within the Transportation & Logistics
sector, and in simple terms, this role means the following: Manages residential and commercial moving
operations. While the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of
daily tasks, technical or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan
around from an early stage. This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to
transportation & logistics because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided
the right foundational skills are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Moving/Relocation Services Manager typically
spends their time applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field,
coordinating with colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job,
and staying current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to transportation & logistics.
Early in a career, most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior
professionals in this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring
junior staff, and sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary
significantly depending on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO,
or as an independent/freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely
consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A practical logistics business opportunity in growing urban Nepal. Beyond that immediate appeal,
this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose
and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the
country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: The rapid rise of e-commerce, food delivery, and cross-border trade is
transforming Nepal's transportation and logistics sector from a purely informal industry into one with real
career structure, professional standards, and growth opportunities. Taken together, these trends suggest
that a career as a Moving/Relocation Services Manager is likely to remain relevant and employable for
the foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating
one's skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Moving/Relocation Services Manager in Nepal generally follows a
fairly predictable progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this
sector. At the entry level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically
expect to earn around NPR 20,500 - 30,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level
professionals in this role tend to earn closer to NPR 40,500 - 65,000 / month. At the senior or specialist
TRANSPORTATION & LOGISTICS
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 1131 of 1372

level — including those who have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their
own independent practice — earnings can rise to NPR 91,000 - 154,500 / month, though reaching this level
naturally takes time, consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Moving/Relocation Services Manager-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi
Arabia): approximately USD 1,300 - 7,250 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD
3,450 - 11,550 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 3,550 - 8,350 / month. As these figures
show, Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad
thanks to zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK,
and Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically
fall in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Moving/Relocation Services Manager, the core skills
generally required include Logistics, Customer Service, and Operations. Beyond these technical or role-
specific skills, most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills,
since almost every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to
colleagues, clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a
genuine willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.
Logistics
Customer Service
Operations
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in transportation & logistics, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger
organizations or fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-
term option.
ENTRY
NPR 20,500 - 30,500 / month
MID
NPR 40,500 - 65,000 / month
SENIOR
NPR 91,000 - 154,500 / month
Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia)

OVERVIEW
A Retail Store Owner is a professional working within the Retail & Sales sector, and in simple terms, this role
means the following: Owns and operates an independent retail business. While the title itself may sound
broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or people-facing skills,
and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage. This career path is
worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to retail & sales because it offers a fairly clear route
from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built during college years and

applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with

usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/

grow alongside traditional shops, creating demand for professionals who understand both in-person
customer service and digital sales channels. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a
Retail Store Owner is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though as with
any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a

clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine
willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.

Communication
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in retail & sales, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations or fully
independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Sales Associate offers a genuinely solid
combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to seek
out an internship, a small real-world project, or direct conversations with working professionals in this
field to get a clearer, first-hand sense of whether this path truly fits your interests and strengths.

427
E-commerce Store Manager
OVERVIEW
A E-commerce Store Manager is a professional working within the Retail & Sales sector, and in simple
terms, this role means the following: Manages an online store's listings, orders, and customer service.
While the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks,
technical or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an
early stage. This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to retail & sales
because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills
are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a E-commerce Store Manager typically spends
their time applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to retail & sales. Early in a career, most
of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in this role
usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A fast-growing career as more Nepali shopping moves online. Beyond that immediate appeal, this
career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose and
visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the country
as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a career like
this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in this field
started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through coursework,
internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Nepal's retail landscape is shifting quickly as e-commerce platforms
grow alongside traditional shops, creating demand for professionals who understand both in-person
customer service and digital sales channels. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a E-
commerce Store Manager is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though
as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying
solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a E-commerce Store Manager in Nepal generally follows a fairly
predictable progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector.
At the entry level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to
earn around NPR 18,500 - 27,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level
professionals in this role tend to earn closer to NPR 39,000 - 62,500 / month. At the senior or specialist level
— including those who have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own
RETAIL & SALES
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 1143 of 1372

independent practice — earnings can rise to NPR 103,500 - 176,000 / month, though reaching this level
naturally takes time, consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a E-
commerce Store Manager-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia):
approximately USD 1,400 - 7,450 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 3,350 -
10,450 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 3,100 - 7,450 / month. As these figures show,
Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to
zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and
Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall
in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a E-commerce Store Manager, the core skills generally
required include E-commerce Platforms, Marketing, and Customer Service. Beyond these technical or
role-specific skills, most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills,
since almost every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to
colleagues, clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a
genuine willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.

approximately USD 1,350 - 7,250 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 3,250 -
10,150 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 3,000 - 7,250 / month. As these figures show,
Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to
zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and
Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall
in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Direct Sales/MLM Consultant, the core skills generally
required include Sales, Networking, and Persistence. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills, most
professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost every role
— even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the
public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep
learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced professionals as being just as
important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree certificate.
Sales
Networking
Persistence
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in retail & sales, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations or fully
independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Direct Sales/MLM Consultant offers a
genuinely solid combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is
usually to seek out an internship, a small real-world project, or direct conversations with working

432
Telemarketing/Call Center Agent
OVERVIEW
A Telemarketing/Call Center Agent is a professional working within the Retail & Sales sector, and in simple
terms, this role means the following: Sells or supports products/services over the phone. While the title
itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or
people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage.
This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to retail & sales because it offers
a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built during
college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Telemarketing/Call Center Agent typically spends
their time applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to retail & sales. Early in a career, most
of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in this role
usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
An accessible entry point into professional sales and customer service. Beyond that immediate
appeal, this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear
purpose and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or
the country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Nepal's retail landscape is shifting quickly as e-commerce platforms
grow alongside traditional shops, creating demand for professionals who understand both in-person
customer service and digital sales channels. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a
Telemarketing/Call Center Agent is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future,
though as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than
relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Telemarketing/Call Center Agent in Nepal generally follows a fairly
predictable progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector.
At the entry level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to
earn around NPR 17,000 - 25,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level
professionals in this role tend to earn closer to NPR 35,500 - 57,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level
— including those who have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own
RETAIL & SALES
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 1157 of 1372

independent practice — earnings can rise to NPR 94,500 - 160,500 / month, though reaching this level
naturally takes time, consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Telemarketing/Call Center Agent-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia):
approximately USD 1,250 - 6,800 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 3,050 -
9,550 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 2,850 - 6,800 / month. As these figures show,
Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to
zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and
Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall
in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Telemarketing/Call Center Agent, the core skills generally
required include Communication, Persistence, and Product Knowledge. Beyond these technical or role-
specific skills, most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills,
since almost every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to
colleagues, clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a
genuine willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.

Real Estate Sales Agent
OVERVIEW
A Real Estate Sales Agent is a professional working within the Retail & Sales sector, and in simple terms,
this role means the following: Sells residential and commercial properties to buyers. While the title itself
may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or people-
facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage. This
career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to retail & sales because it offers a

freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.

— earnings can rise to NPR 81,000 - 138,000 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Insurance Sales Agent-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia):
approximately USD 1,100 - 5,850 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 2,600 -
8,200 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 2,450 - 5,850 / month. As these figures show,
Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to
zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and
Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall
in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Insurance Sales Agent, the core skills generally required
include Sales, Product Knowledge, and Communication. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills,
most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost
every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues,
clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine
willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.
Sales
Product Knowledge
Communication
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in retail & sales, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations or fully
independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.

solid combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to

437
Trade Show/Exhibition Sales Rep
OVERVIEW
A Trade Show/Exhibition Sales Rep is a professional working within the Retail & Sales sector, and in simple
terms, this role means the following: Sells products and services at trade shows and exhibitions. While the
title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or
people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage.
This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to retail & sales because it offers
a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built during
college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Trade Show/Exhibition Sales Rep typically spends
their time applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to retail & sales. Early in a career, most
of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in this role
usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A dynamic, travel-friendly sales career. Beyond that immediate appeal, this career also tends to
offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose and visible impact,
whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the country as a whole. For
students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a career like this does not
require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in this field started with
only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through coursework, internships, and
early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Nepal's retail landscape is shifting quickly as e-commerce platforms
grow alongside traditional shops, creating demand for professionals who understand both in-person
customer service and digital sales channels. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a
Trade Show/Exhibition Sales Rep is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future,
though as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than
relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Trade Show/Exhibition Sales Rep in Nepal generally follows a fairly
predictable progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector.
At the entry level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to
earn around NPR 16,000 - 24,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level
professionals in this role tend to earn closer to NPR 34,500 - 55,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level
— including those who have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own
RETAIL & SALES

independent practice — earnings can rise to NPR 91,000 - 154,500 / month, though reaching this level
naturally takes time, consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.

Real Estate Developer
OVERVIEW
A Real Estate Developer is a professional working within the Real Estate & Property sector, and in simple
terms, this role means the following: Plans and builds residential or commercial property projects. While
the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical
or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage.
This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to real estate & property because
it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built
during college years and early internships.

On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Real Estate Developer typically spends their time
applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with

in cities like Kathmandu and Pokhara are driving steady growth in Nepal's real estate and property
management sector. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a Real Estate Developer is
likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying
competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or
