progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 20,000 - 30,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 46,000 - 73,500 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
REAL ESTATE & PROPERTY
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 1181 of 1372

— earnings can rise to NPR 134,000 - 228,000 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a Land
Surveyor-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia): approximately USD
1,550 - 8,700 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 3,650 - 10,350 / month;
Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 2,900 - 8,100 / month. As these figures show, Gulf countries
generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to zero income
tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and Western Europe)
tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall in between,
combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth remembering that
higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa and relocation costs,
and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Land Surveyor, the core skills generally required include
Surveying, Precision, and Legal Knowledge. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills, most
professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost every role
— even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the
public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep
learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced professionals as being just as
important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree certificate.
Surveying
Precision
Legal Knowledge
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in real estate & property, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations
or fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Land Surveyor offers a genuinely solid
combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to seek
out an internship, a small real-world project, or direct conversations with working professionals in this
field to get a clearer, first-hand sense of whether this path truly fits your interests and strengths.

442
Real Estate Investment Analyst
OVERVIEW
A Real Estate Investment Analyst is a professional working within the Real Estate & Property sector, and in
simple terms, this role means the following: Analyzes property investments for profitability and risk. While
the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical
or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage.
This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to real estate & property because
it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built
during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Real Estate Investment Analyst typically spends
their time applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to real estate & property. Early in a
career, most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior
professionals in this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring
junior staff, and sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary
significantly depending on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO,
or as an independent/freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely
consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A growing finance-adjacent niche within Nepal's real estate boom. Beyond that immediate appeal,
this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose
and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the
country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Rapid urbanization, a growing middle class, and rising apartment living
in cities like Kathmandu and Pokhara are driving steady growth in Nepal's real estate and property
management sector. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a Real Estate Investment
Analyst is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though as with any field,
staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a single
degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Real Estate Investment Analyst in Nepal generally follows a fairly
predictable progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector.
At the entry level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to
earn around NPR 20,000 - 30,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level
professionals in this role tend to earn closer to NPR 45,500 - 73,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level
REAL ESTATE & PROPERTY
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 1183 of 1372

— including those who have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own
independent practice — earnings can rise to NPR 133,000 - 226,000 / month, though reaching this level
naturally takes time, consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.

may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or people-
facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage. This
career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to real estate & property because it

this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in this field
started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through coursework,
internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Rapid urbanization, a growing middle class, and rising apartment living
in cities like Kathmandu and Pokhara are driving steady growth in Nepal's real estate and property
management sector. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a Condo/Apartment
Association Manager is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though as
with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely
on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Condo/Apartment Association Manager in Nepal generally follows a
fairly predictable progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this
sector. At the entry level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically
expect to earn around NPR 23,000 - 34,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level
professionals in this role tend to earn closer to NPR 52,500 - 84,500 / month. At the senior or specialist level
REAL ESTATE & PROPERTY
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 1195 of 1372

— including those who have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own
independent practice — earnings can rise to NPR 153,500 - 261,500 / month, though reaching this level
naturally takes time, consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Condo/Apartment Association Manager-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi
Arabia): approximately USD 1,750 - 10,000 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD
4,150 - 11,850 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 3,300 - 9,300 / month. As these figures
show, Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad
thanks to zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK,
and Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically
fall in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Condo/Apartment Association Manager, the core skills
generally required include Management, Conflict Resolution, and Organization. Beyond these technical or
role-specific skills, most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills,
since almost every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to
colleagues, clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a
genuine willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.
Management
Conflict Resolution
Organization
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in real estate & property, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations
or fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Condo/Apartment Association Manager
offers a genuinely solid combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next

Mortgage/Loan Officer
OVERVIEW
A Mortgage/Loan Officer is a professional working within the Real Estate & Property sector, and in simple
terms, this role means the following: Helps clients secure loans for property purchases. While the title itself
may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or people-
facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage. This
career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to real estate & property because it
offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built
during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Mortgage/Loan Officer typically spends their time
applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to real estate & property. Early in a
career, most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior
professionals in this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring
junior staff, and sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary
significantly depending on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO,
or as an independent/freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely
consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A finance career directly supporting people's homeownership dreams. Beyond that immediate
appeal, this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear
purpose and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or
the country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Rapid urbanization, a growing middle class, and rising apartment living
in cities like Kathmandu and Pokhara are driving steady growth in Nepal's real estate and property
management sector. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a Mortgage/Loan Officer is
likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying
competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or
qualification.

tend to earn closer to NPR 52,000 - 83,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who

OVERVIEW
A Makeup Artist is a professional working within the Beauty & Wellness sector, and in simple terms, this role
means the following: Applies makeup for events, media, or personal clients. While the title itself may sound
broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or people-facing skills,
and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage. This career path is
worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to beauty & wellness because it offers a fairly clear
route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built during college years
and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Makeup Artist typically spends their time applying
specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with colleagues, clients,
supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying current with the latest
tools, standards, or best practices relevant to beauty & wellness. Early in a career, most of the work tends
to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in this role usually take on
responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and sometimes managing
entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending on whether someone
works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/freelance professional,
but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A creative, flexible career with strong wedding-season income potential. Beyond that immediate
appeal, this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear
purpose and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or
the country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Rising disposable income and growing social media influence have
fueled fast growth in Nepal's beauty, grooming, and wellness industry, creating fresh opportunities for
both employed professionals and independent entrepreneurs. Taken together, these trends suggest that
a career as a Makeup Artist is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though
as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying
solely on a single degree or qualification.

Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Makeup Artist in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry

NPR 13,500 - 20,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 31,000 - 50,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice

On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Nail Technician typically spends their time
applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to beauty & wellness. Early in a career,
most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in
this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A low-barrier, steady-income beauty career. Beyond that immediate appeal, this career also tends
to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose and visible impact,
whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the country as a whole. For
students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a career like this does not
require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in this field started with
only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through coursework, internships, and
early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Rising disposable income and growing social media influence have
fueled fast growth in Nepal's beauty, grooming, and wellness industry, creating fresh opportunities for
both employed professionals and independent entrepreneurs. Taken together, these trends suggest that
a career as a Nail Technician is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future,
though as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than
relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Nail Technician in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 13,500 - 20,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 31,000 - 49,500 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
BEAUTY & WELLNESS
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 1208 of 1372

— earnings can rise to NPR 82,500 - 140,500 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a Nail
Technician-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia): approximately USD
1,050 - 6,050 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 2,600 - 7,250 / month;
Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 2,600 - 6,050 / month. As these figures show, Gulf countries
generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to zero income
tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and Western Europe)
tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall in between,
combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth remembering that
higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa and relocation costs,
and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Nail Technician, the core skills generally required include
Nail Art, Precision, and Customer Service. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills, most professionals
who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost every role — even highly
technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the public. Time
management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep learning
throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced professionals as being just as
important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree certificate.
Nail Art
Precision
Customer Service
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in beauty & wellness, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations or
fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Nail Technician offers a genuinely solid
combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to seek
out an internship, a small real-world project, or direct conversations with working professionals in this
field to get a clearer, first-hand sense of whether this path truly fits your interests and strengths.

role means the following: Uses essential oils and scents for therapeutic wellness. While the title itself may
sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or people-facing
skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage. This career path
is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to beauty & wellness because it offers a fairly clear
route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built during college years
and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Aromatherapist typically spends their time
applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to beauty & wellness. Early in a career,
most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in
this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A niche wellness career combining nature with holistic health. Beyond that immediate appeal, this
career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose and
visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the country
as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a career like
this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in this field
started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through coursework,
internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Rising disposable income and growing social media influence have
fueled fast growth in Nepal's beauty, grooming, and wellness industry, creating fresh opportunities for
both employed professionals and independent entrepreneurs. Taken together, these trends suggest that
a career as a Aromatherapist is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future,
though as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than
relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Aromatherapist in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 15,000 - 22,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 34,500 - 55,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
BEAUTY & WELLNESS
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 1220 of 1372

— earnings can rise to NPR 91,500 - 156,000 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Aromatherapist-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia): approximately
USD 1,150 - 6,700 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 2,850 - 8,050 / month;
Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 2,850 - 6,700 / month. As these figures show, Gulf countries
generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to zero income
tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and Western Europe)
tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall in between,
combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth remembering that
higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa and relocation costs,
and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Aromatherapist, the core skills generally required include
Aromatherapy Knowledge, Customer Service, and Wellness Science. Beyond these technical or role-
specific skills, most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills,
since almost every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to
colleagues, clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a
genuine willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.
Aromatherapy Knowledge
Customer Service
Wellness Science
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in beauty & wellness, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations or
fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Aromatherapist offers a genuinely solid
combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to seek
out an internship, a small real-world project, or direct conversations with working professionals in this
field to get a clearer, first-hand sense of whether this path truly fits your interests and strengths.

461
Air Traffic Controller
OVERVIEW
A Air Traffic Controller is a professional working within the Maritime & Aviation sector, and in simple terms,
this role means the following: Manages aircraft movement to ensure safe air travel. While the title itself
may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or people-
facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage. This
career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to maritime & aviation because it
offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built
during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Air Traffic Controller typically spends their time
applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to maritime & aviation. Early in a career,
most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in
this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A high-responsibility, well-paid career keeping the skies safe. Beyond that immediate appeal, this
career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose and
visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the country
as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a career like
this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in this field
started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through coursework,
internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: As a landlocked country, Nepal's maritime opportunities exist mainly
abroad, but its aviation sector — especially mountain flying, helicopter rescue, and growing international
air links — offers genuinely unique, well-paid career paths. Taken together, these trends suggest that a
career as a Air Traffic Controller is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future,
though as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than
relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Air Traffic Controller in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 31,500 - 47,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 84,500 - 135,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
MARITIME & AVIATION
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 1233 of 1372

— earnings can rise to NPR 316,500 - 538,000 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a Air
Traffic Controller-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia): approximately
USD 2,100 - 19,200 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 4,450 - 22,900 / month;
Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 3,500 - 16,450 / month. As these figures show, Gulf countries
generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to zero income
tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and Western Europe)
tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall in between,
combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth remembering that
higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa and relocation costs,
and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Air Traffic Controller, the core skills generally required
include Concentration, Quick Decision-Making, and Communication. Beyond these technical or role-
specific skills, most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills,
since almost every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to
colleagues, clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a
genuine willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.
Concentration
Quick Decision-Making
Communication
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in maritime & aviation, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations or
fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Air Traffic Controller offers a genuinely solid
combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to seek
out an internship, a small real-world project, or direct conversations with working professionals in this
field to get a clearer, first-hand sense of whether this path truly fits your interests and strengths.
ENTRY
NPR 31,500 - 47,500 / month
MID
NPR 84,500 - 135,000 / month
SENIOR
NPR 316,500 - 538,000 / month
Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia)

position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in maritime & aviation, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations or
fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Port Operations Manager offers a
genuinely solid combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is
usually to seek out an internship, a small real-world project, or direct conversations with working

466
Drone Traffic/Airspace Manager
OVERVIEW
A Drone Traffic/Airspace Manager is a professional working within the Maritime & Aviation sector, and in
simple terms, this role means the following: Manages growing commercial drone traffic in shared
airspace. While the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily
tasks, technical or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around
from an early stage. This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to maritime
& aviation because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right
foundational skills are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Drone Traffic/Airspace Manager typically spends
their time applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to maritime & aviation. Early in a career,
most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in
this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A brand-new aviation career emerging alongside the drone industry boom. Beyond that immediate
appeal, this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear
purpose and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or
the country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: As a landlocked country, Nepal's maritime opportunities exist mainly
abroad, but its aviation sector — especially mountain flying, helicopter rescue, and growing international
air links — offers genuinely unique, well-paid career paths. Taken together, these trends suggest that a
career as a Drone Traffic/Airspace Manager is likely to remain relevant and employable for the
foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's
skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Drone Traffic/Airspace Manager in Nepal generally follows a fairly
predictable progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector.
At the entry level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to
earn around NPR 34,000 - 51,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level
professionals in this role tend to earn closer to NPR 90,500 - 144,500 / month. At the senior or specialist
level — including those who have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their
MARITIME & AVIATION
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 1246 of 1372

own independent practice — earnings can rise to NPR 338,500 - 575,500 / month, though reaching this
level naturally takes time, consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Drone Traffic/Airspace Manager-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia):
approximately USD 2,250 - 20,550 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 4,750 -
24,500 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 3,700 - 17,600 / month. As these figures show,
Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to
zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and
Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall
in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Drone Traffic/Airspace Manager, the core skills generally
required include Airspace Regulations, Technology, and Coordination. Beyond these technical or role-
specific skills, most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills,
since almost every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to
colleagues, clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a
genuine willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.

in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Power Plant Operator, the core skills generally required
include Technical Skill, Safety, and Attention to Detail. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills, most
professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost every role
— even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the
public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep
learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced professionals as being just as
important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree certificate.
Technical Skill
Safety
Attention to Detail
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in energy & utilities, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations or fully
independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Power Plant Operator offers a genuinely
solid combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to
seek out an internship, a small real-world project, or direct conversations with working professionals in
this field to get a clearer, first-hand sense of whether this path truly fits your interests and strengths.

471
Grid/Transmission Engineer
OVERVIEW
A Grid/Transmission Engineer is a professional working within the Energy & Utilities sector, and in simple
terms, this role means the following: Designs and maintains the electricity transmission network. While the
title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or
people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage.
This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to energy & utilities because it
offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built
during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Grid/Transmission Engineer typically spends their
time applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to energy & utilities. Early in a career,
most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in
this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A critical engineering career as Nepal expands its power infrastructure. Beyond that immediate
appeal, this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear
purpose and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or
the country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Nepal's enormous hydropower potential, combined with a national push
toward electrification and renewable energy, is making the energy and utilities sector one of the more
strategically important growth areas in the economy. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career
as a Grid/Transmission Engineer is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future,
though as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than
relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Grid/Transmission Engineer in Nepal generally follows a fairly
predictable progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector.
At the entry level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to
earn around NPR 25,000 - 38,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level
professionals in this role tend to earn closer to NPR 47,500 - 75,500 / month. At the senior or specialist level
— including those who have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own
ENERGY & UTILITIES
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 1258 of 1372

independent practice — earnings can rise to NPR 105,000 - 178,500 / month, though reaching this level
naturally takes time, consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a Grid/
Transmission Engineer-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia):
approximately USD 1,900 - 9,550 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 4,400 -
11,350 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 3,800 - 10,250 / month. As these figures show,
Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to
zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and
Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall
in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Grid/Transmission Engineer, the core skills generally
required include Electrical Engineering, Systems Design, and Safety. Beyond these technical or role-
specific skills, most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills,
since almost every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to
colleagues, clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a
genuine willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.
Electrical Engineering
Systems Design
Safety

against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a Utility
Line Worker-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia): approximately USD
1,900 - 9,550 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 4,400 - 11,300 / month;
Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 3,750 - 10,200 / month. As these figures show, Gulf countries
generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to zero income
tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and Western Europe)
tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall in between,
combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth remembering that
higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa and relocation costs,
and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Utility Line Worker, the core skills generally required include
Electrical Skills, Physical Fitness, and Safety. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills, most
professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost every role
— even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the
public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep
learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced professionals as being just as
important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree certificate.
Electrical Skills
Physical Fitness
Safety
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in energy & utilities, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations or fully
independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Utility Line Worker offers a genuinely solid
combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to seek
out an internship, a small real-world project, or direct conversations with working professionals in this
field to get a clearer, first-hand sense of whether this path truly fits your interests and strengths.

476
Water Utility Manager
OVERVIEW
A Water Utility Manager is a professional working within the Energy & Utilities sector, and in simple terms,
this role means the following: Manages a city or region's water supply infrastructure. While the title itself
may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or people-
facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage. This
career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to energy & utilities because it offers a
fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills are built during
college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Water Utility Manager typically spends their time
applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to energy & utilities. Early in a career,
most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in
this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A public-service career directly ensuring clean water access. Beyond that immediate appeal, this
career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose and
visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the country
as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a career like
this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in this field
started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through coursework,
internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Nepal's enormous hydropower potential, combined with a national push
toward electrification and renewable energy, is making the energy and utilities sector one of the more
strategically important growth areas in the economy. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career
as a Water Utility Manager is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though
as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying
solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Water Utility Manager in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 26,500 - 40,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 50,000 - 80,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
ENERGY & UTILITIES
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 1270 of 1372

— earnings can rise to NPR 111,000 - 189,000 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Water Utility Manager-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia):
approximately USD 2,000 - 10,100 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 4,650 -
12,000 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 4,000 - 10,850 / month. As these figures show,
Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to
zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and
Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall
in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Water Utility Manager, the core skills generally required
include Operations Management, Engineering Knowledge, and Public Service. Beyond these technical or
role-specific skills, most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills,
since almost every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to
colleagues, clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a
genuine willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.

SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a NGO Program Manager in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 25,000 - 38,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 50,500 - 80,500 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
NON-PROFIT & SOCIAL SERVICES
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 1282 of 1372

have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
— earnings can rise to NPR 131,000 - 223,000 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a NGO
Program Manager-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia):
approximately USD 1,500 - 7,850 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 3,350 -
8,800 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 3,000 - 7,600 / month. As these figures show,
Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to
zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and
Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall
in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a NGO Program Manager, the core skills generally required
include Program Management, Communication, and Grant Writing. Beyond these technical or role-
specific skills, most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills,
since almost every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to
colleagues, clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a
genuine willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.
Program Management
Communication
Grant Writing
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in non-profit & social services, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger
organizations or fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-
term option.
ENTRY
NPR 25,000 - 38,000 / month
MID
NPR 50,500 - 80,500 / month
SENIOR
NPR 131,000 - 223,000 / month
Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia)
USD 1,500 - 7,850 / month
USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe
USD 3,350 - 8,800 / month
Australia & New Zealand
USD 3,000 - 7,600 / month

For students currently weighing their options, a career as a NGO Program Manager offers a genuinely
solid combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to
seek out an internship, a small real-world project, or direct conversations with working professionals in
this field to get a clearer, first-hand sense of whether this path truly fits your interests and strengths.

481
Social Worker
OVERVIEW
A Social Worker is a professional working within the Non-Profit & Social Services sector, and in simple
terms, this role means the following: Supports vulnerable individuals and families through social services.
While the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks,
technical or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an
early stage. This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to non-profit & social
services because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right
foundational skills are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Social Worker typically spends their time applying
specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with colleagues, clients,
supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying current with the latest
tools, standards, or best practices relevant to non-profit & social services. Early in a career, most of the
work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in this role
usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A deeply meaningful career directly improving people's lives. Beyond that immediate appeal, this
career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose and
visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the country
as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a career like
this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in this field
started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through coursework,
internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Nepal has one of the highest concentrations of NGOs and INGOs per
capita in the world, and this sector consistently ranks among the better-paying options for skilled,
English-fluent professionals passionate about social impact. Taken together, these trends suggest that a
career as a Social Worker is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though as
with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely
on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Social Worker in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 26,000 - 39,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 52,000 - 83,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
NON-PROFIT & SOCIAL SERVICES
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 1285 of 1372

— earnings can rise to NPR 135,000 - 229,500 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.

— even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the
public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep
learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced professionals as being just as
important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree certificate.

WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A deeply important career safeguarding society's most vulnerable members. Beyond that
immediate appeal, this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense
of clear purpose and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities,
companies, or the country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth
noting that a career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many
professionals in this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually
through coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Nepal has one of the highest concentrations of NGOs and INGOs per
capita in the world, and this sector consistently ranks among the better-paying options for skilled,
English-fluent professionals passionate about social impact. Taken together, these trends suggest that a
career as a Child Protection Officer is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future,
though as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than
relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Child Protection Officer in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 25,000 - 37,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 50,000 - 80,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
NON-PROFIT & SOCIAL SERVICES
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 1296 of 1372

have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
— earnings can rise to NPR 130,000 - 221,000 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a Child
Protection Officer-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia): approximately
USD 1,500 - 7,800 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 3,300 - 8,700 / month;
Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 3,000 - 7,550 / month. As these figures show, Gulf countries
generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to zero income
tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and Western Europe)
tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall in between,
combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth remembering that
higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa and relocation costs,
and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Child Protection Officer, the core skills generally required
include Child Welfare Knowledge, Empathy, and Investigation. Beyond these technical or role-specific
skills, most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost
every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues,
clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine
willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.
Child Welfare Knowledge
Empathy
Investigation
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in non-profit & social services, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger
organizations or fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-
term option.

solid combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to

Refugee/Migration Support Worker
OVERVIEW
A Refugee/Migration Support Worker is a professional working within the Non-Profit & Social Services
sector, and in simple terms, this role means the following: Assists refugees and migrants with resettlement
and support services. While the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific
combination of daily tasks, technical or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students
can plan around from an early stage. This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are
drawn to non-profit & social services because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment,
provided the right foundational skills are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Refugee/Migration Support Worker typically
spends their time applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field,
coordinating with colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job,
and staying current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to non-profit & social
services. Early in a career, most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while
more senior professionals in this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making,
mentoring junior staff, and sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can
vary significantly depending on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an
NGO, or as an independent/freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains
largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A meaningful career supporting some of the world's most vulnerable people. Beyond that immediate
appeal, this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear
purpose and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or
the country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.

capita in the world, and this sector consistently ranks among the better-paying options for skilled,
English-fluent professionals passionate about social impact. Taken together, these trends suggest that a
career as a Refugee/Migration Support Worker is likely to remain relevant and employable for the
foreseeable future, though as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's
skills rather than relying solely on a single degree or qualification.
