ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 1310 of 1372

490
Grant Writer
OVERVIEW
A Grant Writer is a professional working within the Non-Profit & Social Services sector, and in simple terms,
this role means the following: Writes funding proposals to secure grants for organizations. While the title
itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or
people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage.
This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to non-profit & social services
because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills
are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Grant Writer typically spends their time applying
specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with colleagues, clients,
supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying current with the latest
tools, standards, or best practices relevant to non-profit & social services. Early in a career, most of the
work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior professionals in this role
usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring junior staff, and
sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary significantly depending
on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO, or as an independent/
freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A specialized writing career keeping vital non-profit work funded. Beyond that immediate appeal,
this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose
and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the
country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Nepal has one of the highest concentrations of NGOs and INGOs per
capita in the world, and this sector consistently ranks among the better-paying options for skilled,
English-fluent professionals passionate about social impact. Taken together, these trends suggest that a
career as a Grant Writer is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though as
with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely
on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Grant Writer in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 23,000 - 35,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 46,500 - 74,500 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
NON-PROFIT & SOCIAL SERVICES
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 1311 of 1372

— earnings can rise to NPR 121,000 - 205,500 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Grant Writer-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia): approximately USD
1,400 - 7,250 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 3,050 - 8,100 / month;
Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 2,800 - 7,000 / month. As these figures show, Gulf countries
generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to zero income
tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and Western Europe)
tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall in between,
combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth remembering that
higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa and relocation costs,
and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Grant Writer, the core skills generally required include
Writing, Research, and Attention to Detail. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills, most professionals
who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost every role — even highly
technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the public. Time
management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep learning
throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced professionals as being just as
important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree certificate.
Writing
Research
Attention to Detail
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in non-profit & social services, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger
organizations or fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-
term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Grant Writer offers a genuinely solid
combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to seek
out an internship, a small real-world project, or direct conversations with working professionals in this
field to get a clearer, first-hand sense of whether this path truly fits your interests and strengths.

MID
NPR 46,500 - 74,500 / month
SENIOR
NPR 121,000 - 205,500 / month
Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia)

OVERVIEW
A Elected Political Representative is a professional working within the Politics & Public Policy sector, and in
simple terms, this role means the following: Represents constituents in local, provincial, or federal
government. While the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of
daily tasks, technical or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan
around from an early stage. This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to

common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in politics & public policy, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations
or fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Policy Researcher/Think Tank Analyst offers
a genuinely solid combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is

495
Political Journalist/Analyst
OVERVIEW
A Political Journalist/Analyst is a professional working within the Politics & Public Policy sector, and in
simple terms, this role means the following: Reports on and analyzes political events and policy. While the
title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical or
people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage.
This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to politics & public policy
because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills
are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Political Journalist/Analyst typically spends their
time applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to politics & public policy. Early in a
career, most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior
professionals in this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring
junior staff, and sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary
significantly depending on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO,
or as an independent/freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely
consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A career keeping citizens informed about power and governance. Beyond that immediate appeal,
this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose
and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the
country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Nepal's evolving federal democratic structure, with elections at the local,
provincial, and national level, creates a genuinely broad range of career paths for those interested in
shaping public policy and governance. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a Political
Journalist/Analyst is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though as with
any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a
single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Political Journalist/Analyst in Nepal generally follows a fairly
predictable progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector.
At the entry level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to
earn around NPR 24,000 - 36,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level
professionals in this role tend to earn closer to NPR 48,000 - 76,500 / month. At the senior or specialist level
POLITICS & PUBLIC POLICY
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 1325 of 1372

— including those who have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own
independent practice — earnings can rise to NPR 134,000 - 227,500 / month, though reaching this level
naturally takes time, consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Political Journalist/Analyst-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia):
approximately USD 1,700 - 8,700 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 3,650 -
11,450 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 3,450 - 8,700 / month. As these figures show,
Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to
zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and
Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall
in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Political Journalist/Analyst, the core skills generally
required include Writing, Research, and Political Knowledge. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills,
most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost
every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues,
clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine
willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.
Writing
Research
Political Knowledge

in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Political Speechwriter, the core skills generally required
include Writing, Political Knowledge, and Persuasion. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills, most
professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost every role
— even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the
public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep
learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced professionals as being just as
important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree certificate.
Writing
Political Knowledge
Persuasion

Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in politics & public policy, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger organizations
or fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-term option.
For students currently weighing their options, a career as a Political Speechwriter offers a genuinely
solid combination of stability, growth potential, and meaningful work. The best next step is usually to

500
Public Policy Consultant
OVERVIEW
A Public Policy Consultant is a professional working within the Politics & Public Policy sector, and in simple
terms, this role means the following: Advises governments or organizations on effective policy design.
While the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks,
technical or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an
early stage. This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to politics & public
policy because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational
skills are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Public Policy Consultant typically spends their
time applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to politics & public policy. Early in a
career, most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior
professionals in this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring
junior staff, and sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary
significantly depending on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO,
or as an independent/freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely
consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A strategic career translating research into real-world government action. Beyond that immediate
appeal, this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear
purpose and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or
the country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: Nepal's evolving federal democratic structure, with elections at the local,
provincial, and national level, creates a genuinely broad range of career paths for those interested in
shaping public policy and governance. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a Public
Policy Consultant is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though as with
any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a
single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Public Policy Consultant in Nepal generally follows a fairly predictable
progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector. At the entry
level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to earn around
NPR 27,000 - 40,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals in this role
tend to earn closer to NPR 54,000 - 86,500 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including those who
POLITICS & PUBLIC POLICY
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 1340 of 1372

have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent practice
— earnings can rise to NPR 151,500 - 257,500 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes time,
consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Public Policy Consultant-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia):
approximately USD 1,950 - 9,850 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 4,100 -
12,950 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 3,900 - 9,850 / month. As these figures show,
Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to
zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and
Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall
in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Public Policy Consultant, the core skills generally required
include Policy Analysis, Communication, and Research. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills, most
professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost every role
— even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues, clients, or the
public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine willingness to keep
learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced professionals as being just as
important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree certificate.
Policy Analysis
Communication
Research

those who have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent
practice — earnings can rise to NPR 45,500 - 77,500 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes
time, consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Community Interfaith Coordinator-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi
Arabia): approximately USD 650 - 3,550 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD
2,300 - 5,450 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 2,000 - 4,750 / month. As these figures
show, Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad
thanks to zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK,
and Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically
fall in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Community Interfaith Coordinator, the core skills generally
required include Communication, Cultural Sensitivity, and Organization. Beyond these technical or role-
specific skills, most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills,
since almost every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to
colleagues, clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a
genuine willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.
Communication
Cultural Sensitivity
Organization
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in religious & community services, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger
organizations or fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-
term option.

505
Religious Education Teacher
OVERVIEW
A Religious Education Teacher is a professional working within the Religious & Community Services sector,
and in simple terms, this role means the following: Teaches religious studies and spiritual education. While
the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very specific combination of daily tasks, technical
or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that students can plan around from an early stage.
This career path is worth exploring seriously for students who are drawn to religious & community services
because it offers a fairly clear route from education to employment, provided the right foundational skills
are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Religious Education Teacher typically spends
their time applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to religious & community services. Early
in a career, most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior
professionals in this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring
junior staff, and sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary
significantly depending on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO,
or as an independent/freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely
consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
A meaningful career passing down cultural and spiritual heritage. Beyond that immediate appeal,
this career also tends to offer something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose
and visible impact, whether that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the
country as a whole. For students who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a
career like this does not require having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in
this field started with only a general interest and built specific expertise gradually through
coursework, internships, and early job experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: As the birthplace of Lord Buddha and home to deep Hindu cultural
traditions, Nepal offers unique, culturally significant career paths in religious and community service that
remain socially respected across generations. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a
Religious Education Teacher is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though
as with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying
solely on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Religious Education Teacher in Nepal generally follows a fairly
predictable progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector.
At the entry level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to
earn around NPR 11,000 - 16,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level professionals
in this role tend to earn closer to NPR 24,000 - 38,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level — including
RELIGIOUS & COMMUNITY SERVICES
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 1355 of 1372

those who have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own independent
practice — earnings can rise to NPR 54,000 - 92,000 / month, though reaching this level naturally takes
time, consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.

CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: As the birthplace of Lord Buddha and home to deep Hindu cultural
traditions, Nepal offers unique, culturally significant career paths in religious and community service that
remain socially respected across generations. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a
Religious Artifact Restorer is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though as
with any field, staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely
on a single degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Religious Artifact Restorer in Nepal generally follows a fairly
predictable progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector.
At the entry level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to
earn around NPR 10,000 - 15,500 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level
professionals in this role tend to earn closer to NPR 22,500 - 36,000 / month. At the senior or specialist level
RELIGIOUS & COMMUNITY SERVICES
ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 1367 of 1372

— including those who have built strong reputations, taken on leadership responsibilities, or run their own
independent practice — earnings can rise to NPR 51,000 - 86,500 / month, though reaching this level
naturally takes time, consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.
SALARY ABROAD — INTERNATIONAL COMPARISON
Salary Abroad — International Comparison: One of the most important things students should understand
about this career is that compensation abroad is not a single fixed number — it varies quite significantly
depending on which country or region someone works in, and it is misleading to compare Nepal only
against one country like the UK or the US alone. Based on current 2025-2026 salary survey data for a
Religious Artifact Restorer-equivalent role internationally: Gulf Countries (UAE, Qatar, Saudi Arabia):
approximately USD 700 - 3,950 / month; USA, Canada, UK & Western Europe: approximately USD 2,550 -
6,100 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 2,250 - 5,300 / month. As these figures show,
Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to
zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and
Western Europe) tend to offer the highest overall compensation, and Australia/New Zealand typically fall
in between, combining strong wages with a high but manageable cost of living. It's also worth
remembering that higher foreign salaries usually come with a correspondingly higher cost of living, visa
and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work
abroad.
SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Religious Artifact Restorer, the core skills generally required
include Restoration Techniques, Art History, and Precision. Beyond these technical or role-specific skills,
most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills, since almost
every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to colleagues,
clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a genuine
willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.
Restoration Techniques
Art History
Precision
CAREER GROWTH PATH
Career Growth Path: Most students entering this field can expect to start in a junior or assistant-level
position, gradually taking on more independent responsibility over the first two to four years. From there,
common next steps include moving into specialist, senior, or supervisory roles, and for many professionals
in religious & community services, an eventual path toward either leadership positions within larger
organizations or fully independent practice, consulting, or entrepreneurship becomes a realistic long-
term option.

ScorpStudy Career Explorer | Page 1369 of 1372

510
Farmer / Crop Cultivator
OVERVIEW
A Farmer / Crop Cultivator is a professional working within the Agriculture & Environment sector, and in
simple terms, this role means the following: Grows crops, raises livestock, and manages farmland to
produce food and raw materials. While the title itself may sound broad, in practice it involves a very
specific combination of daily tasks, technical or people-facing skills, and a defined career trajectory that
students can plan around from an early stage. This career path is worth exploring seriously for students
who are drawn to agriculture & environment because it offers a fairly clear route from education to
employment, provided the right foundational skills are built during college years and early internships.
ROLES & RESPONSIBILITIES
On a practical, day-to-day level, someone working as a Farmer / Crop Cultivator typically spends their
time applying specialized knowledge to solve real problems relevant to their field, coordinating with
colleagues, clients, supervisors, or the public depending on the exact nature of the job, and staying
current with the latest tools, standards, or best practices relevant to agriculture & environment. Early in a
career, most of the work tends to be more hands-on and closely supervised, while more senior
professionals in this role usually take on responsibilities involving planning, decision-making, mentoring
junior staff, and sometimes managing entire teams or projects. The exact daily routine can vary
significantly depending on whether someone works for a government office, a private company, an NGO,
or as an independent/freelance professional, but the underlying core skill set required remains largely
consistent.
WHY CHOOSE THIS CAREER
The single most essential job in human history, and still one of the most needed careers in
agriculture-dependent Nepal. Beyond that immediate appeal, this career also tends to offer
something many students genuinely value: a sense of clear purpose and visible impact, whether
that impact is on individual clients, communities, companies, or the country as a whole. For students
who are still exploring different options, it is worth noting that a career like this does not require
having everything figured out immediately — many professionals in this field started with only a
general interest and built specific expertise gradually through coursework, internships, and early job
experience.
CURRENT SCOPE & INDUSTRY TRENDS
Current Scope & Industry Trends: As a country where agriculture remains a major part of the economy,
and where climate vulnerability is a growing national concern, Nepal is placing more emphasis on
modern farming techniques, environmental protection, and sustainable resource management, creating
fresh opportunities in this space. Taken together, these trends suggest that a career as a Farmer / Crop
Cultivator is likely to remain relevant and employable for the foreseeable future, though as with any field,
staying competitive will require continuously updating one's skills rather than relying solely on a single
degree or qualification.
SALARY IN NEPAL
Salary in Nepal: Compensation for a Farmer / Crop Cultivator in Nepal generally follows a fairly
predictable progression tied to experience level, based on current 2026 salary survey data for this sector.
At the entry level, fresh graduates or those with less than two years of experience can typically expect to

earn around NPR 19,500 - 29,000 / month. With several years of relevant experience, mid-level

naturally takes time, consistent skill development, and often a track record of proven results.

7,850 / month; Australia & New Zealand: approximately USD 2,900 - 6,900 / month. As these figures show,
Gulf countries generally offer the most accessible entry point for Nepali workers moving abroad thanks to
zero income tax and employer-sponsored housing, while Western countries (USA, Canada, UK, and

and relocation costs, and time away from family, all of which should factor into any decision to work

SKILLS & QUALIFICATIONS NEEDED
Skills & Qualifications Needed: To succeed as a Farmer / Crop Cultivator, the core skills generally required
include Crop Management, Physical Endurance, and Weather Awareness. Beyond these technical or role-
specific skills, most professionals who do well in this career also develop strong communication skills,
since almost every role — even highly technical ones — eventually requires explaining ideas clearly to
colleagues, clients, or the public. Time management, adaptability to new tools or regulations, and a
genuine willingness to keep learning throughout one's career are also consistently cited by experienced
professionals as being just as important as the formal technical qualifications listed on a degree
certificate.
